title.zh: 卷四十八 楊李翟應霍爰徐列傳
title.en: Volume 48: Biographies of Yang, Li, Di, Ying, Huo, Yuan, Xu
title.raw: 卷四十八 楊李翟應霍爰徐列傳 Volume 48: Biographies of Yang, Li, Di, Ying, Huo, Yuan, Xu

sentences.zh: 楊終字子山，蜀郡成都人也。

translations.literal: Yang Zhong, courtesy name Zishan, was a man of Chengdu in Shu commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Zhong, styled Zishan, came from Chengdu in Shu commandery.

sentences.zh: 年十三，為郡小吏，太守奇其才，遣詣京師受業，習春秋。

translations.literal: At age thirteen he became a petty clerk in the commandery; the grand administrator marveled at his talent and sent him to the capital to study, where he learned the Chunqiu.
translations.idiomatic: When he was thirteen he served as a junior clerk in the commandery. The grand administrator, struck by his ability, sent him to the capital for schooling, and there he mastered the Spring and Autumn Annals.

sentences.zh: [一]顯宗時，征詣蘭台，拜校書郎。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Ming [note 1], he was summoned to the Orchid Terrace and appointed collator.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Ming he was called to the Orchid Terrace and given the post of collating secretary [1].

sentences.zh: 注[一]袁山松書曰：「時蜀郡有雷震決曹，終上白記，以為斷獄煩苛所致，太守乃令終賦雷電之意，而奇之也。」

translations.literal: Note [1] Yuan Shansong's book says: "At that time in Shu commandery lightning struck the bureau of decisions; Zhong submitted a memorial arguing that it was caused by vexatious severity in judging cases, whereupon the grand administrator ordered Zhong to compose on the meaning of thunder and lightning, and marveled at him."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Yuan Shansong writes that when lightning in Shu commandery struck the office that handled legal decisions, Yang Zhong submitted a written report blaming harsh and tangled litigation; the grand administrator then had him compose a piece on thunder and lightning and was greatly impressed.

sentences.zh: 注[一]春秋：「昭公二十年，曹公孫會自鄸出奔宋。

translations.literal: Note [1] Chunqiu: "In the twentieth year of Duke Zhao, Gongsun Hui of Cao fled from Mao to Song."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The Spring and Autumn records that in Duke Zhao's twentieth year Gongsun Hui of Cao left Mao and fled to Song.

sentences.zh: 」公羊傳曰：「畔也。

translations.literal: The Gongyang commentary says: "It means rebellion."
translations.idiomatic: The Gongyang commentary glosses the wording as indicating rebellion.

sentences.zh: 曷為不言畔？

translations.literal: Why does it not say "rebel"?
translations.idiomatic: Why does the text avoid saying outright that he rebelled?

sentences.zh: 為公子喜時之後諱也。

translations.literal: It is to avoid naming the posterity of Duke Xishi.
translations.idiomatic: The wording spares the descendants of Prince Xishi.

sentences.zh: 春秋為賢者諱也。

translations.literal: The Chunqiu avoids naming faults for the worthy.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn spares the worthy by concealing their shame.

sentences.zh: 何賢乎公子喜時？

translations.literal: In what was Prince Xishi worthy?
translations.idiomatic: What made Prince Xishi worthy?

sentences.zh: 讓國也。

translations.literal: He yielded the state.
translations.idiomatic: He gave up his claim to the rulership.

sentences.zh: 君子善善也長，惡惡也短，惡惡止其身，善善及子孫。

translations.literal: The gentleman: to praise good is long-lasting; to blame evil is brief; blaming evil stops with the person himself; praising good reaches to his descendants.
translations.idiomatic: The gentleman prolongs praise of the good and keeps censure of the wicked short; he limits blame to the individual but lets praise extend to posterity.

sentences.zh: 賢者子孫，故君子為之諱。」

translations.literal: The descendants of the worthy—therefore the gentleman conceals it for them."
translations.idiomatic: Because they are heirs of a worthy man, the gentleman shields their fault in the record."

sentences.zh: 注[二]前書音義曰：「父族、母族、妻族也。」

translations.literal: Note [2] The Former Han's "Sounds and Meanings" says: "The father's kin, the mother's kin, and the wife's kin."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: The Qian Han shu "Yinyi" defines the three clans as the father's, the mother's, and the wife's lines.

sentences.zh: 注[三]太宗，文帝也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Taizong is Emperor Wen [of Han].
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Taizong is Emperor Wen of the Former Han.

sentences.zh: 史記曰：「文帝德至盛也，豈不仁哉。

translations.literal: The Shiji says: "Emperor Wen's virtue reached the utmost—how could he not be benevolent?
translations.idiomatic: The Records of the Historian says of Emperor Wen that his virtue rose to perfection—could he have been anything but humane?

sentences.zh: 」除去收孥相坐之律也。

translations.literal: He abolished the statutes on collective punishment and imprisonment of dependents."
translations.idiomatic: He did away with laws that seized kin and punished whole households for one person's crime."

sentences.zh: 注[四]「□」字或作「牛」。

translations.literal: Note [4] The character in "□" is sometimes written "ox."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: The lacuna in the text is sometimes written with the character for "ox."

sentences.zh: 疫，病也。

translations.literal: Yi means illness.
translations.idiomatic: Yi here means pestilence or epidemic disease.

sentences.zh: 注[五]元帝詔曰「安土重遷，黎人之性」也。

translations.literal: Note [5] Emperor Yuan's edict said "To cling to one's soil and shrink from moving is the nature of the common people."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: Emperor Yuan's edict quotes the saying that people naturally cling to their land and dread uprooting.

sentences.zh: 注[六]尚書盤庚序曰：「盤庚五遷，將治亳，殷人咨胥怨。

translations.literal: Note [6] The "Pan Geng" preface to the Shang shu says: "Pan Geng moved five times; about to settle at Bo, the Yin people all murmured and complained."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [6]: The preface to "Pan Geng" in the Documents says Pan Geng relocated the capital five times; as he was about to rule from Bo, the Yin people grumbled together.

sentences.zh: 」亳，今河南偃師，故曰「近遷洛邑」。

translations.literal: Bo is today's Yanshi in Henan—hence it says "near moving to Luoyi."
translations.idiomatic: Bo is present-day Yanshi in Henan, which is why the gloss speaks of a move close to Luoyi.

sentences.zh: 注[七]毛，草也。

translations.literal: Note [7] Mao means grass.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [7]: Mao means vegetation or grassland.

sentences.zh: 爾雅曰：「孤竹、北戶、西王母、日下謂之四荒。

translations.literal: The Erya says: "Guzhu, Beihu, the Queen Mother of the West, and Rixia are called the four wastes."
translations.idiomatic: The Erya lists Guzhu, Beihu, the Queen Mother of the West, and Rixia as the "four outer wastes."

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「東至於泰遠，西至於邠國，南至於濮鈆，北至於祝栗，謂之四極。

translations.literal: It also says: "East to Taiyuan, west to Bin, south to Puyin, north to Zhuli—these are called the four extremes."
translations.idiomatic: It also defines the four extremes as reaching east to Taiyuan, west to Bin, south to Puyin, and north to Zhuli.

sentences.zh: 」言不毛、荒極，直論遠耳，非必此地也。

translations.literal: Saying "not grass" and "wastes and extremes" is only speaking of distance in general; it need not be these exact places."
translations.idiomatic: Calling a place "without grass" or placing it among the wastes and extremes is a way of saying "very far away," not a claim that it lies exactly at those named points.

sentences.zh: 注[八]元帝初元三年，珠崖郡反，待詔賈捐之以為宜□珠崖，救人飢餓，乃罷珠崖郡。

translations.literal: Note [8] In Emperor Yuan's first year Chuyuan 3, Zhuya commandery rebelled; awaiting-orders Jia Juanzhi held that Zhuya should be abandoned to save people from starvation, and Zhuya commandery was then abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [8]: In the third year of Chuyuan under Emperor Yuan, Zhuya commandery rose in revolt; Jia Juanzhi, holding the rank of awaiting orders, argued for abandoning Zhuya to relieve famine, and the commandery was abolished.

sentences.zh: 光武二十一年，鄯善、車師王等十六國皆遣子入侍，請都護。

translations.literal: In Guangwu's twenty-first year, the kings of Shanshan, Jushi, and sixteen other states all sent sons as hostages and asked for a protector-general.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-first year of Emperor Guangwu, the rulers of Shanshan, Jushi, and sixteen other polities sent their sons to court as hostages and begged for a protector-general.

sentences.zh: 帝以中國初定，未遑外事，還其侍子，厚加賞賜。

translations.literal: The emperor, because the interior was only just settled and he had no leisure for outer affairs, returned their hostages with rich gifts.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor, with China still consolidating and no margin for frontier entanglements, sent the hostages home and showered them with rewards.

sentences.zh: 介鱗喻遠夷，言其人與魚□無異也。

translations.literal: Scales and shellfish are a metaphor for distant barbarians, meaning their people differ no more from fish and shellfish.
translations.idiomatic: The image of fish and scaly creatures stands for distant tribes whose inhabitants are treated as little different from sea life.

sentences.zh: 衣裳謂中國也。

translations.literal: Garments mean the Central States.
translations.idiomatic: By "robes and caps" the text means the Chinese heartland.

sentences.zh: 楊雄法言曰：「珠崖之絕，捐之之力也，否則鱗介易我衣裳。」

translations.literal: Yang Xiong's Fa yan says: "The severing of Zhuya was Juanzhi's doing; otherwise fish-scales would have traded places with our robes and caps."
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xiong writes in the Fa yan that cutting off Zhuya was Juanzhi's achievement; otherwise the barbarians might have overturned Chinese rule.

sentences.zh: 注[九]公羊傳曰「毀泉台何以書？

translations.literal: Note [9] The Gongyang says: "Why record the destruction of the spring platform?
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [9]: The Gongyang asks why the annals record the razing of the Spring Platform.

sentences.zh: 譏爾。

translations.literal: To criticize."
translations.idiomatic: To register blame."

sentences.zh: 築之譏，毀之譏，先祖為之而己毀之，勿居而已」也。

translations.literal: Building it is criticized; destroying it is criticized—the ancestors built it and you yourself destroy it: do no more than not dwell in it."
translations.idiomatic: Both raising and tearing it down draw censure: the ancestors built it, yet later generations tear it down; the proper course is simply not to live there."

sentences.zh: 注[一〇]公羊傳曰：「襄公十一年作三軍。

translations.literal: Note [10] The Gongyang says: "In Duke Xiang's eleventh year three armies were created.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [10]: The Gongyang records that in Duke Xiang's eleventh year three armies were established.

sentences.zh: 三軍者何？

translations.literal: What are the three armies?
translations.idiomatic: What did "three armies" signify?

sentences.zh: 三卿也。

translations.literal: Three ministers."
translations.idiomatic: They corresponded to three high ministers.

sentences.zh: 」昭公五年傳曰：「捨中軍。

translations.literal: The fifth year of Duke Zhao commentary says: "They abolished the central army."
translations.idiomatic: The Gongyang on Duke Zhao's fifth year adds that the central army was later disbanded.

sentences.zh: 捨中軍者何？

translations.literal: What does abolishing the central army mean?
translations.idiomatic: What did abolishing the central army mean?

sentences.zh: 復古也。

translations.literal: Restoring the ancient model."
translations.idiomatic: A return to the older military organization."

sentences.zh: 」言捨之與留，量時制宜也。

translations.literal: Thus whether to set them aside or keep them depended on adapting to the times.
translations.idiomatic: Keeping or abolishing those forces was a matter of suiting institutions to the moment.

sentences.zh: 終又言：「宣帝博征腢儒，論定五經於石渠閣。

translations.literal: Zhong also said: "Emperor Xuan widely summoned Confucian scholars and fixed the meaning of the Five Classics in the Stone Canal Pavilion.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Zhong urged that Emperor Xuan had summoned scholars broadly and settled the Five Classics at the Stone Canal Pavilion.

sentences.zh: 方今天下少事，學者得成其業，而章句之徒，破壞大體。

translations.literal: Now the realm has few troubles and students can complete their studies, yet the chapter-and-verse crowd ruin the great pattern.
translations.idiomatic: Today, with few disturbances in the empire, learners could perfect their craft, but petty exegetes are tearing apart the larger tradition.

sentences.zh: 宜如石渠故事，永為後世則。

translations.literal: It would be fitting to follow the Stone Canal precedent and make a lasting model for later ages."
translations.idiomatic: He proposed repeating the Stone Canal conference so as to leave a permanent standard for posterity."

sentences.zh: 」於是詔諸儒於白虎觀論考同異焉。

translations.literal: Thereupon an edict ordered the scholars to debate agreements and differences at the White Tiger Hall.
translations.idiomatic: An edict followed, summoning Confucians to the White Tiger Hall to thrash out textual agreement and disagreement.

sentences.zh: 會終坐事系獄，博士趙博、校書郎班固、賈逵等，以終深曉春秋，學多異聞，表請之，終又上書自訟，即日貰出，乃得與於白虎觀焉。

translations.literal: It happened that Zhong was imprisoned for an offense; Erudite Zhao Bo, collator Ban Gu, Jia Kui, and others, because Zhong thoroughly understood the Chunqiu and had learned many unusual traditions, memorialized for him; Zhong also submitted a memorial in his own defense, and the same day he was pardoned and released, and so was able to take part in the White Tiger conference.
translations.idiomatic: When Yang Zhong was jailed for a separate matter, Zhao Bo, Ban Gu, Jia Kui, and others petitioned that his mastery of the Annals and wide learning deserved mercy; his own memorial won him a pardon that very day, and he joined the White Tiger deliberations.

sentences.zh: [一]後受詔刪太史公書為十餘萬言。

translations.literal: Later he received orders to abridge the Grand Scribe's book to over a hundred thousand characters.
translations.idiomatic: He was later commissioned to condense Sima Qian's history to more than one hundred thousand characters.

sentences.zh: 注[一]與音預。

translations.literal: Note [1] Yu is read like yu meaning "participate."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The gloss gives the reading yu (fourth tone), the same sound as in the word meaning to take part in."

sentences.zh: 時太后兄□尉馬廖，謹篤自守，不訓諸子。

translations.literal: At that time the Empress Dowager's elder brother Commandant of the Guards Ma Liao was cautious and self-contained but did not instruct his sons.
translations.idiomatic: Ma Liao, elder brother of the empress dowager and commandant of the guards, lived carefully and frugally for himself yet failed to discipline his children.

sentences.zh: 終與廖交善，以書戒之曰：「終聞堯舜之民，可比屋而封；

translations.literal: Zhong was on good terms with Liao and wrote to admonish him, saying: "I have heard that the people under Yao and Shun were such that one could enfeoff them house by house;
translations.idiomatic: Yang Zhong, who was friendly with Ma Liao, sent a letter of warning: "They say that under Yao and Shun every household deserved a noble's patent;

sentences.zh: 桀紂之民，可比屋而誅。

translations.literal: the people under Jie and Zhou were such that one could execute them house by house.
translations.idiomatic: under Jie and Zhou every household would have deserved the executioner."

sentences.zh: [一]何者？

translations.literal: [1] Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why was that?

sentences.zh: 堯舜為之堤防，桀紂示之驕奢故也。

translations.literal: Yao and Shun set dikes for them; Jie and Zhou showed them arrogance and extravagance."
translations.idiomatic: Yao and Shun built moral dikes around the people, while Jie and Zhou taught arrogance and waste."

sentences.zh: 詩曰：『皎皎練絲，在所染之。

translations.literal: The Odes say: "Bright white the silk thread—what it becomes depends on the dye.
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Poetry says, "Gleaming white silk—its color depends on what dye it meets."

sentences.zh: 』[二]上智下愚，謂之不移；

translations.literal: [2] The highest wise and lowest stupid are called unchangeable;
translations.idiomatic: The brightest and the dullest are said to be beyond reshaping;

sentences.zh: 中庸之流，要在教化。

translations.literal: the middle sort depend on transformation.
translations.idiomatic: those in between rise or fall through education.

sentences.zh: 春秋殺太子母弟，直稱君甚惡之者，坐失教也。

translations.literal: When the Chunqiu records the killing of an heir or younger brother by title alone, naming the ruler alone, it greatly blames him—for losing instruction."
translations.idiomatic: When the Spring and Autumn names only the ruler for killing an heir or full brother, it heaps blame on him for failing to teach."

sentences.zh: [三]禮制，人君之子年八歲，為置少傅，教之書計，以開其明；

translations.literal: [3] By ritual, when a ruler's son reaches eight sui, a junior tutor is appointed to teach writing and reckoning and open his understanding;
translations.idiomatic: Rite prescribes that at eight a prince receives a junior tutor in literacy and arithmetic to awaken his mind;

sentences.zh: [四]十五置太傅，教之經典，以道其志。

translations.literal: [4] at fifteen a grand tutor is appointed to teach the classics and guide his aims.
translations.idiomatic: at fifteen a grand tutor instructs him in the canon to set his purpose straight."

sentences.zh: 漢興，諸侯王不力教誨，多觸禁忌，故有亡國之禍，而乏嘉善之稱。

translations.literal: Since Han rose, many feudal kings did not exert themselves in teaching, repeatedly violated prohibitions, and therefore had the misfortune of losing their states and lacked a fair reputation.
translations.idiomatic: Since the Han founding, many kings have neglected moral instruction, stumbled into taboos, lost their fiefs, and won no good name.

sentences.zh: 今君位地尊重，海內所望，豈可不臨深履薄，以為至戒！

translations.literal: Now your position and standing are weighty and the realm looks to you—how can you not tread as on ice or cliff-edge and take that as your supreme warning!
translations.idiomatic: Your rank is exalted and all eyes are on you; you must walk as if on thin ice or a precipice and make that your chief rule!

sentences.zh: 黃門郎年幼，血氣方盛，[五]既無長君退讓之風，[六]而要結輕狡無行之客，縱而莫誨，視成任性，[七]鑒念前往，可為寒心。

translations.literal: The gentlemen of the yellow gates are young; their blood and qi are just strong; [5] they lack the yielding manner of Elder Prince Changjun; [6] yet they befriend slippery, worthless guests; indulged without admonition, they look on formed habits and follow whim; [7] reflecting on what has gone before should make one's blood run cold.
translations.idiomatic: The young gentlemen at the Yellow Gates are in their vigorous years; they have none of Elder Prince Changjun's modest reserve, yet they bind themselves to reckless companions. Left unchecked they grow willful. One glance at past examples is enough to freeze the heart.

sentences.zh: 君侯誠宜以臨深履薄為戒。

translations.literal: My lord should truly take treading deep water and thin ice as your warning.
translations.idiomatic: You, my lord, ought to live by that same warning of ice and abyss."

sentences.zh: 」廖不納。

translations.literal: Liao did not accept it.
translations.idiomatic: Ma Liao ignored the advice.

sentences.zh: 子豫後坐縣書誹謗，[八]廖以就國。

translations.literal: His son Yu later was convicted for posting slanderous writings; [8] Liao was ordered to his kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: His son Ma Yu was later condemned for displaying libelous placards, and Ma Liao was sent back to his fief.

sentences.zh: 注[一]事見陸賈新語。

translations.literal: Note [1] The matter appears in Lu Jia's Xin yu.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The comparison appears in Lu Jia's New Sayings.

sentences.zh: 注[二]逸詩也。

translations.literal: Note [2] It is a lost ode.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: The line comes from a poem not preserved in the received Odes.

sentences.zh: 皎皎，白貌也。

translations.literal: Jiaojiao means a white appearance.
translations.idiomatic: Jiaojiao describes a gleaming white look.

sentences.zh: 墨子曰：「墨子見染絲者歎曰：『染於蒼則蒼，染於黃則黃，故染不可不慎也。』」

translations.literal: Mozi says: "Mozi saw one who dyed silk and sighed, saying: 'Dyed with indigo it turns indigo, dyed with yellow it turns yellow—therefore one must be careful in dyeing.'"
translations.idiomatic: Mozi tells of watching silk being dyed and sighing that indigo turns it blue and yellow turns it yellow, so the dyer's choice matters fatally."

sentences.zh: 注[三]公羊傳曰：「晉侯殺其太子申生。

translations.literal: Note [3] The Gongyang says: "The Marquis of Jin killed his heir Shengsheng.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: The Gongyang asks why the text says the marquis of Jin killed Crown Prince Shensheng.

sentences.zh: 曷為直稱晉侯？

translations.literal: Why is the Marquis of Jin named straight?
translations.idiomatic: Why name the ruler of Jin alone?

sentences.zh: 曰以殺其太子母弟，直稱君者甚之也。」

translations.literal: It says: because in killing his heir and younger brother by full blood, to name the ruler alone is to blame him heavily."
translations.idiomatic: The answer: naming only the lord when he kills his heir or full brother heaps the guilt on him."

sentences.zh: 注[四]大戴禮曰：「古者八歲出就外捨，學小蓺焉，履小節焉。

translations.literal: Note [4] The Da Dai li says: "In antiquity at eight sui they went out to the outer lodge, learned minor arts, and practiced minor observances."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: The Elder Dai record says children at eight went to outer quarters for minor skills and etiquette.

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「為置三少，曰少保、少傅、少師，是與太子宴者也。

translations.literal: It also says: "Three juniors were appointed for them, called junior guardian, junior tutor, junior instructor—these feast with the heir."
translations.idiomatic: It adds that three "junior" officers—the junior guardian, tutor, and instructor—kept company with the heir apparent.

sentences.zh: 」禮記內則曰「十年出就外傅，居宿於外學書計」也。

translations.literal: The Neize chapter of the Liji says "at ten they go out to the outer tutor, lodge outside, and learn writing and reckoning."
translations.idiomatic: The "Inner Rules" in the Record of Rites sets study abroad with writing and reckoning at ten.

sentences.zh: 注[五]廖子防及光俱為黃門郎。

translations.literal: Note [5] Liao's sons Fang and Guang were both gentlemen of the yellow gates.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: Ma Liao's sons Fang and Guang both held posts as gentlemen of the Yellow Gates.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰「及其壯也，血氣方剛，戒之在□」也。

translations.literal: Confucius said "In his prime, blood and qi were just strong—guard against combat."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius warned that in the vigor of life one must beware strife."

sentences.zh: 注[六]文帝竇後兄長君，弟廣國字少君，此兩人所出微，絳、灌等選長者之有節行者與之居，長君、少君由此為退讓君子，不敢以富貴驕人也。

translations.literal: Note [6] Emperor Wen's Empress Dou's elder brother was Elder Prince Changjun; her younger brother Guangguo, styled Shaojun, came from humble origins; Zhou Bo and Guan Ying and others chose elders of principled conduct to live with them, and Changjun and Shaojun thereby became yielding gentlemen who did not presume on wealth and rank.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [6]: Dou Changjun, elder brother of Emperor Wen's empress, and Dou Guangguo (Shaojun), though of low birth, were paired with steady mentors by Zhou Bo and Guan Ying and grew into modest gentlemen who never flaunted their new rank.

sentences.zh: 注[七]馬防傳曰「兄弟貴盛，賓客奔湊，四方畢至，數百餘人皆為食客」也。

translations.literal: Note [7] Ma Fang's biography says "the brothers were noble and powerful; guests flocked from every quarter; several hundred all became retainers."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [7]: Ma Fang's biography notes hundreds of hangers-on thronging the brothers' gates.

sentences.zh: 注[八]縣音懸。

translations.literal: Note [8] Xian is read like xuan meaning "suspend."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [8]: The character xian (county) is here read xuan, meaning "to display in public" or "to suspend."

sentences.zh: 終兄鳳為郡吏，太守廉范為州所考，遣鳳候終，終為范遊說，坐徙北地。

translations.literal: Zhong's elder brother Feng was a commandery clerk; when Grand Administrator Lian Fan was investigated by the province, he sent Feng to wait on Zhong, and Zhong pleaded for Fan on his behalf and was banished to Beidi for it.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Zhong's brother Feng served the commandery. When Grand Administrator Lian Fan fell under provincial investigation, Feng visited Yang Zhong, who spoke up for Fan and was exiled to Beidi for meddling.

sentences.zh: [一]帝東巡狩，鳳皇黃龍並集，終讚頌嘉瑞，上述祖宗鴻業，凡十五章，奏上，詔貰還故郡。

translations.literal: [1] When the emperor toured east, phoenix and yellow dragon appeared together; Zhong composed hymns praising the auspicious omens and narrating the great enterprise of the former emperors—fifteen pieces in all—which he presented; an edict pardoned him and sent him back to his home commandery.
translations.idiomatic: On the emperor's eastern progress, phoenix and yellow dragon appeared; Yang Zhong offered fifteen odes celebrating the portents and the ancestral achievement, received a pardon, and was allowed home.

sentences.zh: 著春秋外傳十二篇，改定章句十五萬言。

translations.literal: He wrote twelve chapters of "Outer Commentary" on the Chunqiu and revised glosses to 150,000 characters.
translations.idiomatic: He authored twelve fascicles of outer commentary on the Spring and Autumn and revised chapter-and-verse commentary to one hundred fifty thousand characters.

sentences.zh: 永元十二年，征拜郎中，以病卒。

translations.literal: In Yongyuan 12 he was summoned and appointed gentleman of the palace; he died of illness.
translations.idiomatic: In the twelfth year of Yongyuan he was called to office as a gentleman of the palace and died in that post.

translations.literal: Endnote marker [2].
translations.idiomatic: The chapter ends this entry with commentary note two.

sentences.zh: 注[一]益部耆舊傳曰「終徙於北地望松縣，而母於蜀物故。

translations.literal: Note [1] The Yibu qijiu zhuan says "Zhong was moved to Wangsong county in Beidi, while his mother died in Shu.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Local tradition says Yang Zhong was banished to Wangsong in Beidi while his mother died in Shu.

sentences.zh: 終自傷被罪充邊，乃作晨風之詩以舒其憤」也。

translations.literal: Zhong, grieving that he had been convicted and sent to the border, composed the poem "Morning Wind" to vent his resentment."
translations.idiomatic: He wrote the "Morning Breeze" poem to voice his bitterness at punishment and exile."

sentences.zh: 注[二]袁山松書曰「侍中賈逵薦終博達忠直，征拜郎中。

translations.literal: Note [2] Yuan Shansong's book says "Palace attendant Jia Kui recommended Zhong as broadly learned, loyal, and upright; he was summoned as gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Yuan Shansong records Jia Kui's recommendation and Yang Zhong's summons as gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 及卒，賜錢二十萬」也。

translations.literal: When he died, two hundred thousand cash were granted for his funeral."
translations.idiomatic: At his death the court granted two hundred thousand cash for burial expenses."

sentences.zh: 李法字伯度，漢中南鄭人也。

translations.literal: Li Fa, courtesy name Bodu, was a man of Nan Zheng in Hanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: Li Fa, styled Bodu, came from Nanzheng in Hanzhong commandery.

sentences.zh: 博通腢書，性剛而有節。

translations.literal: He was broadly versed in the classics and by nature stern yet principled.
translations.idiomatic: He read widely in the canon and was upright by temperament, with a strong sense of duty.

sentences.zh: 和帝永元九年，應賢良方正對策，除博士，遷侍中、光祿大夫。

translations.literal: In Emperor He's Yongyuan 9 he answered the summons for worthy and upright and was appointed erudite, then promoted attendant-in-chamber and supernumerary grandee.
translations.idiomatic: In the ninth year of Yongyuan under Emperor He he took the worthy-and-upright examination, became an erudite, and rose to palace attendant and supernumerary grandee of the Household.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，上疏以為朝政苛碎，違永平、建初故事；

translations.literal: After more than a year he submitted a memorial holding that court government was harsh and minute, departing from the precedents of Yongping and Jianchu;
translations.idiomatic: A year later he memorialized that government had grown harsh and fussy, straying from the Yongping and Jianchu models;

sentences.zh: 宦官權重，椒房寵盛；

translations.literal: eunuch power was heavy and favor in the pepper chambers excessive;
translations.idiomatic: eunuchs wielded too much power and the imperial in-laws enjoyed excessive favor;

sentences.zh: 又譏史官記事不實，後世有識，尋功計德，必不明信。

translations.literal: he also criticized the historians for not recording truthfully—later generations with insight, seeking merit and assessing virtue, would surely lack clear trust.
translations.idiomatic: he charged the historiographers with falsifying the record, so that later readers weighing deeds would find no clear warrant.

sentences.zh: 坐失旨，下有司，免為庶人。

translations.literal: For missing the emperor's intent he was handed to the authorities and dismissed as commoner.
translations.idiomatic: He missed the sovereign's meaning, was referred to judicial officials, and reduced to commoner status.

sentences.zh: 還鄉里，杜門自守。

translations.literal: He returned to his village and shut his gate, keeping to himself.
translations.idiomatic: He went home, barred his gate, and lived in seclusion.

sentences.zh: 故人儒生時有候之者，言談之次，問其不合上意之由，法未嘗應對。

translations.literal: Former friends and scholars sometimes visited; in conversation they asked the reason he had not matched the emperor's wishes, and Fa never answered.
translations.idiomatic: Old acquaintances and students would call; when talk turned to why he had offended the throne, he would not reply.

sentences.zh: 友人固問之，法曰：「鄙夫可與事君乎哉？

translations.literal: When his friends pressed him firmly, Fa said, "Can a vulgar fellow serve his lord?"
translations.idiomatic: Pressed until he spoke, Fa answered with a line from the Analects: "Can a worthless man be trusted to serve his prince?"

sentences.zh: 苟患失之，無所不至。

translations.literal: If one fears losing his place, there is nothing to which he will not descend.
translations.idiomatic: Men who dread the loss of office will stop at nothing.

sentences.zh: [一]孟子有言：『夫仁者如射，正己而後發。

translations.literal: [1] Mencius has a saying: 'The humane man is like an archer: he sets himself straight and only then lets fly.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Mencius compares the humane man to an archer who straightens his stance before he looses the string.

sentences.zh: 發而不中，不怨勝己者，反諸身而已矣。

translations.literal: If he shoots and misses, he does not resent those who surpass him; he turns the fault back on himself, and that is all.'
translations.idiomatic: When his shot goes wide, he blames no rival who outshoots him; he looks to his own form alone.

sentences.zh: 』」[二]在家八年，征拜議郎、諫議大夫，正言極辭，無改於舊。

translations.literal: '" [2] At home eight years, he was summoned and appointed gentleman consultant and remonstrance grandee; he spoke with the utmost blunt criticism and did not alter his old manner.
translations.idiomatic: After eight years at home the court called him back as a gentleman consultant and remonstrance grandee; he still spoke his mind as sharply as before.

sentences.zh: 出為汝南太守，政有聲多。

translations.literal: He went out as grand administrator of Runan; his administration had a notable reputation.
translations.idiomatic: As grand administrator of Runan he governed so well that his name was widely praised.

sentences.zh: 後歸鄉里，卒於家。

translations.literal: Later he returned to his village and died at home.
translations.idiomatic: He eventually retired to his home district and died there.

sentences.zh: 注[一]此以上論語孔子之言也。

translations.literal: Note [1] The foregoing is Confucius's words in the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The lines above quote Confucius from the Analects.

sentences.zh: 鄭玄注云：「無所不至謂諂佞邪媚，無所不為也。」

translations.literal: Zheng Xuan's commentary says: 'Nothing to which he will not descend means fawning, toadying, and wicked flattery—there is nothing such men will not do.'
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan glosses the phrase as describing sycophants who will commit any baseness.

sentences.zh: 注[二]孟子公孫丑篇之言也。

translations.literal: Note [2] These are words from the Gongsun Chou chapter of Mencius.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: The passage comes from the Gongsun Chou section of Mencius.

sentences.zh: 反諸身而已，言克己自責，不責人也。

translations.literal: 'Turn it back on oneself only' means to restrain oneself and blame oneself, not to blame others.
translations.idiomatic: To turn the matter back on oneself is to practice self-restraint and self-reproach rather than faulting others.

sentences.zh: 翟酺字子超，廣漢雒人也。

translations.literal: Zhai Pu, courtesy name Zichao, was a man of Luo in Guanghan.
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Pu, styled Zichao, came from Luo in Guanghan commandery.

sentences.zh: [一]四世傳詩。

translations.literal: [1] For four generations his family transmitted the Odes.
translations.idiomatic: Poetry had been the family learning for four generations.

sentences.zh: 酺好老子，尤善圖緯、天文、歷筭。

translations.literal: Pu loved the Laozi and was especially skilled in prognostic charts, astronomy, and calendrical calculation.
translations.idiomatic: He favored the Laozi and excelled at apocryphal charts, astronomy, and calendar mathematics.

sentences.zh: 以報舅讎，當徙日南，亡於長安，為卜相工，後牧羊涼州。

translations.literal: For avenging his uncle's enemy he should have been banished to Rinan; he fled to Chang'an and worked as a diviner and physiognomer, later herding sheep in Liangzhou.
translations.idiomatic: After killing a man to avenge his uncle he faced exile to Rinan, fled to Chang'an, earned a living as a fortune-teller and face-reader, and later tended sheep in Liangzhou.

sentences.zh: 遇赦還。

translations.literal: He met an amnesty and returned.
translations.idiomatic: An amnesty allowed him to come home.

sentences.zh: 仕郡，征拜議郎，遷侍中。

translations.literal: He served the commandery, was summoned as gentleman consultant, and promoted to palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: He held commandery office, then received appointment as gentleman consultant and rose to palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 注[一]雒屬廣漢郡，漳山雒水所出，南入湔，故城在今雒縣南。

translations.literal: Note [1] Luo belonged to Guanghan commandery; the Luo River issues from Mount Zhang; southward it enters Jian; the old city lay south of present-day Luo county.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Luo was in Guanghan; the river rises on Mount Zhang and flows south into Jian; the old walled town stood south of modern Luo county.

sentences.zh: 湔音子田反。

translations.literal: Jian is read with the fanqie zi-tian.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss gives the fanqie spelling zi-tian for the name Jian.

sentences.zh: 時尚書有缺，詔將大夫六百石以上試對政事、天文、道術，以高第者補之。

translations.literal: At that time the secretariat had a vacancy; an edict ordered generals and grandees from six hundred bushels upward to be examined on state affairs, astronomy, and Daoist arts, and those of highest rank were to fill the post.
translations.idiomatic: A Secretariat seat was open; the emperor told officers at six hundred bushels and above to sit an examination on government, astronomy, and occult learning, with top scorers to be appointed.

sentences.zh: 酺自恃能高，而忌故太史令孫懿，恐其先用，乃往候懿。

translations.literal: Pu, trusting in his high ability, envied the former grand astrologer Sun Yi and feared Yi would be used first; he therefore went to call on Yi.
translations.idiomatic: Confident in his own talent, Zhai Pu resented the former grand astrologer Sun Yi and feared Yi would be chosen ahead of him, so he paid Yi a visit.

sentences.zh: 既坐，言無所及，唯涕泣流連。

translations.literal: When they had sat down, he spoke of nothing else—only wept without cease.
translations.idiomatic: Once seated he wept continuously and said nothing else.

sentences.zh: 懿怪而問之，酺曰：「圖書有漢賊孫登，將以才智為中官所害。

translations.literal: Yi, wondering, asked him; Pu said, 'The charts and books record a Han traitor named Sun Deng who will be ruined by inner-court eunuchs because of his talents and wisdom.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Yi asked why; Zhai Pu claimed the prognostications named a rebel Sun Deng who would be destroyed by eunuchs on account of his cleverness.

sentences.zh: 觀君表相，似當應之。

translations.literal: Looking at your outer appearance, you seem destined to match that prophecy.'
translations.idiomatic: From your looks, he said, Yi seemed the man the prophecy described.

sentences.zh: [一]酺受恩接，淒愴君之禍耳！

translations.literal: [1] I, Pu, having received your kindness, only grieve bitterly at the disaster coming upon you!'
translations.idiomatic: [1] He added that, owing to Yi's kindness to him, he wept for the calamity about to strike Yi.

sentences.zh: 」懿憂懼，移病不試。

translations.literal: Yi, worried and afraid, submitted a sick note and did not take the examination.
translations.idiomatic: Terrified, Sun Yi filed a illness report and skipped the exam.

sentences.zh: [二]由是酺對第一，拜尚書。

translations.literal: [2] Thereby Pu ranked first in the examination and was appointed secretariat director.
translations.idiomatic: [2] With Yi out of the running, Zhai Pu took first place and became a Secretariat director.

sentences.zh: 注[一]春秋保干圖曰「漢賊臣，名孫登，大形小口，長七尺九寸，巧用法，多技方，詩書不用，賢人杜口」也。

translations.literal: Note [1] The Chunqiu Baogan tu says: 'Han will have a traitorous minister named Sun Deng, large in frame, small of mouth, seven chi nine cun in height; clever at using law, with many crafty stratagems; he will not need the Odes and Documents, and worthies will fall silent.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: An apocryphal chart describes a Han traitor Sun Deng—tall, small-mouthed, legally shrewd, contemptuous of the classics, and silencing good men.

sentences.zh: 注[二]移病謂作文移而稱病也。

translations.literal: Note [2] 'Submit a sick note' means to compose a memorial shifting blame and plead illness.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: 'Shifting illness' meant filing a memorial pleading sickness to withdraw.

sentences.zh: 時安帝始親政事，追感祖母宋貴人，悉封其家。

translations.literal: At that time Emperor An had begun to handle government in person; remembering his feelings for his grandmother the honorable Lady Song, he ennobled her whole family.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor An had just begun to rule in his own name; moved by memory of his grandmother Lady Song, he showered titles on her kin.

sentences.zh: 又元舅耿寶及皇后兄弟閻顯等並用威權。

translations.literal: Further his maternal uncle Geng Bao and the empress's brothers Yan Xian and company all wielded awesome power together.
translations.idiomatic: His uncle Geng Bao and the Yan brothers, the empress's siblings, likewise monopolized frightening influence.

sentences.zh: 酺上疏諫曰：

translations.literal: Pu submitted a memorial remonstrating, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Pu therefore offered a long memorial of remonstrance.

sentences.zh: 臣聞微子佯狂而去殷，叔孫通背秦而歸漢，彼非自□其君，時不可也。

translations.literal: I have heard that Weizi feigned madness and left Yin, and Shusun Tong turned his back on Qin and returned to Han—those men did not despise their lords; the times would not allow it.
translations.idiomatic: He cited Weizi, who feigned madness to flee Yin, and Shusun Tong, who abandoned Qin for Han—not because they scorned their rulers, but because the age forced their hand.

sentences.zh: 臣荷殊絕之恩，蒙值不諱之政，豈敢雷同受寵，而以戴天履地。

translations.literal: I bear an extraordinary grant of favor and encounter a government that does not forbid plain speech—how dare I echo others to receive favor while I tread heaven and stand on earth?
translations.idiomatic: He had received exceptional favor under a court that tolerated frank speech; he could not parrot the crowd for profit while claiming to stand between heaven and earth.

sentences.zh: [一]伏惟陛下應天履祚，歷值中興，當建太平之功，而未聞致化之道。

translations.literal: [1] I humbly reflect that Your Majesty received the mandate from Heaven and occupies the throne, passing through the restoration—we ought to achieve the work of great peace, yet I have not heard the way to bring transformation.
translations.idiomatic: [1] The emperor bore Heaven's mandate and had survived the restoration; he should be building an era of supreme peace, yet no path to true reform was visible.

sentences.zh: 蓋遠者難明，請以近事征之。

translations.literal: Remote matters are hard to clarify; I beg to verify them by a recent example.
translations.idiomatic: Distant precedents were obscure; he asked to prove his point with recent history.

sentences.zh: 昔竇、鄧之寵，傾動四方，兼官重紱，盈金積貨，至使議弄神器，改更社稷。

translations.literal: Formerly the favor shown the Dou and Deng clans rocked the four quarters; they piled concurrent offices and multiple seals, piled gold and hoarded goods, until counselors toyed with the sacred regalia and wished to alter the altars of soil and grain.
translations.idiomatic: The Dou and Deng affines had once tilted the realm, stacking offices, seals, gold, and goods until advisers meddled with the throne itself and plotted to shift the dynastic altars.

sentences.zh: [二]豈不以埶尊威廣，以致斯患乎？

translations.literal: [2] Was it not because their power was lofty and their might spread far that this disaster arose?
translations.idiomatic: [2] Had not their exalted station and wide reach bred that catastrophe?

sentences.zh: 及其破壞，頭顙墯地，願為孤豚，豈可得哉！

translations.literal: When they were broken, heads and skulls smashed to the ground—who then would wish to be a lone piglet, yet how could it be obtained!
translations.idiomatic: When those houses fell, heads rolled like melons—who would not then trade rank for the life of a lone pig, if only he could?

sentences.zh: [三]夫致貴無漸失必暴，受爵非道殃必疾。

translations.literal: [3] Honor reached without gradual steps must be lost in violence; rank received by improper ways brings disaster swiftly.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Sudden greatness ends in violent ruin; rank gained by crooked paths draws swift punishment.

sentences.zh: 今外戚寵幸，功均造化，漢元以來，未有等比。

translations.literal: Now the favor granted outer kin equals the transforming work of nature; since Emperor Yuan of Han there has been no parallel.
translations.idiomatic: The present in-laws enjoyed favor rivaling nature's own shaping power—unmatched since Emperor Yuan's reign.

sentences.zh: 陛下誠仁恩周洽，以親九族。

translations.literal: Your Majesty truly extends humane grace everywhere and cherishes the nine degrees of kin by affection.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor's kindness truly embraced the wider kinship network.

sentences.zh: 然祿去公室，政移私門，覆車重尋，寧無摧折。

translations.literal: Yet salaries have left the ducal house and policy passed into private gates; the overturned cart is sought again—can there be no shattering?
translations.idiomatic: Yet revenue and power had drained from the public fisc into private mansions; to repeat the overturned-cart mistake invited another smash-up.

sentences.zh: [四]而朝臣在位，莫肯正議，翕翕訾訾，更相佐附。

translations.literal: [4] Yet court ministers in their posts are unwilling to speak upright blame; they buzz and whisper, lending one another mutual support.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Meanwhile ministers stayed mute or murmured approval, bolstering one another's cowardice.

sentences.zh: [五]臣恐威權外假，歸之良難，虎翼一奮，卒不可制。

translations.literal: [5] I fear that if awesome power is lent abroad, getting it back will be hard indeed; once the tiger grows wings it can no longer be controlled at the end.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Authority delegated to outsiders might never be reclaimed; a tiger given wings could not be caged afterward.

sentences.zh: [六]故孔子曰「吐珠於澤，誰能不含」；

translations.literal: [6] Hence Confucius said, 'When pearls are spat into the marsh, who can keep from taking them in his mouth?'
translations.idiomatic: [6] Confucius had warned that once power was scattered like pearls in a marsh, every hand would seize them.

sentences.zh: [七]老子稱「國之利器，不可以示人」。

translations.literal: [7] Laozi says, 'The sharp tools of the state must not be shown to others.'
translations.idiomatic: [7] Laozi forbade flaunting the state's keen instruments.

sentences.zh: [八]此最安危*[之極]*戒，社稷之深計也。

translations.literal: [8] This is the utmost warning touching safety and peril, a deep plan for the altars of soil and grain.
translations.idiomatic: [8] That, he concluded, was the crux of survival or ruin for the dynasty.

sentences.zh: 注[一]雷之發聲，物皆同應，言無是非者謂之雷同。

translations.literal: Note [1] When thunder sounds, things all echo alike—calling men who have no right or wrong 'echoing thunder.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Ministers who chimed together regardless of truth were likened to mindless thunder-echoes.

sentences.zh: 禮記曰：「無雷同。

translations.literal: The Record of Rites says: 'Do not echo thunder.'
translations.idiomatic: The Record of Rites enjoins men not to mimic empty thunder.

sentences.zh: 」左傳曰「君履后土而戴皇天」也。

translations.literal: The Zuo Commentary says, 'The lord treads on the rear earth while he bears the august heaven on his crown.'
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Tradition speaks of the ruler who stands on earth yet bears heaven on his head—his solemn charge.

sentences.zh: 注[二]神器謂天位也。

translations.literal: Note [2] 'Sacred regalia' means the throne of heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: 'Sacred regalia' denoted the imperial throne.

sentences.zh: 老子曰：「天下神器，不可為也。

translations.literal: Laozi says: 'The sacred instruments of All under Heaven must not be acted upon.'
translations.idiomatic: Laozi says the realm's sacred vessels cannot be handled like private tools.

sentences.zh: 」竇憲出入禁中，得幸太後，圖為殺害。

translations.literal: Dou Xian went in and out of the forbidden palace, won the empress dowager's favor, and plotted murder.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Xian had haunted the inner palace, won the empress dowager's trust, and conspired to kill.

sentences.zh: 帝知其謀，誅之。

translations.literal: The emperor learned of the plot and executed him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor uncovered the plot and put him to death.

sentences.zh: 鄧太后崩，宮人告鄧悝、鄧弘等取廢帝故事，謀立平原王得。

translations.literal: When Empress Dowager Deng died, palace women reported that Deng Kui, Deng Hong, and others had taken the precedent of the deposed emperor and plotted to set up the prince of Pingyuan, De.
translations.idiomatic: After Empress Dowager Deng's death, palace informants charged Deng Kui and Deng Hong with copying the deposed emperor's coup and backing Prince De of Pingyuan.

sentences.zh: 帝聞，遂免鄧氏為庶人也。

translations.literal: The emperor heard of it and thereupon reduced the Dengs to commoner status.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor believed the tale and stripped the Dengs to commoners.

sentences.zh: 注[三]莊子曰，或聘莊子，莊子謂其使曰：「子見夫犧牛乎？

translations.literal: Note [3] Zhuangzi says: Someone tried to hire Zhuangzi; Zhuangzi told his messenger, 'Have you seen the sacrificial ox?
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Zhuangzi's parable of the fattened ox begins with a hiring offer.

sentences.zh: 衣以文繡，食以芻菽。

translations.literal: It is clothed in figured embroidery and fed beans and hay.
translations.idiomatic: The beast wears brocade and eats grain and fodder.

sentences.zh: 及其牽而入於太廟，欲為孤犢，其可得乎？

translations.literal: But when they lead it into the great temple—who then would wish to be a lone calf, yet how could one get that?'
translations.idiomatic: Dragged to the great temple, it would gladly trade places with any scrawny calf—if only it could.

sentences.zh: 」此作「豚」，不同也。

translations.literal: Here the text writes 'piglet,' which differs.
translations.idiomatic: The Hou Han shu text reads 'piglet' instead of 'calf,' a variant wording.

sentences.zh: 注[四]賈誼曰「諺雲前車覆，後車誡」也。

translations.literal: Note [4] Jia Yi said, 'The proverb runs: The front cart overturns—the rear cart is warned.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: Jia Yi quoted the proverb about learning from a predecessor's wreck.

sentences.zh: 注[五]詩小雅曰：「翕翕訾訾，亦孔之哀。

translations.literal: Note [5] An ode in the Shijing minor odes says: 'Buzzing and whispering—how greatly to be pitied.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: The Shijing laments ministers buzzing and backbiting.

sentences.zh: 」毛傳曰：「翕翕然患其上，訾訾然不思稱職。

translations.literal: The Mao commentary says: 'Buzzing means fretting at one's superiors; whispering means not thinking about performing one's duty.'
translations.idiomatic: Mao's gloss reads the lines as grumbling against superiors while neglecting duty.

sentences.zh: 」爾雅曰：「翕翕，訾訾，莫供職也。

translations.literal: The Erya says: 'Buzzing and whispering mean failing to serve in office.'
translations.idiomatic: The Erya defines the same phrases as dereliction of office.

sentences.zh: 」訾音將徙反。

translations.literal: Zi is read with the fanqie jiang-xi.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary gives fanqie jiang-xi for the reading of zi.

sentences.zh: 「訿」與「訾」古字通。

translations.literal: The ancient graphs for zi and zi were interchangeable.
translations.idiomatic: Two homophone graphs were used interchangeably in antiquity.

sentences.zh: 注[六]韓詩外傳曰：「無為虎傅翼，將飛入邑，擇人而食。

translations.literal: Note [6] The Han shi waizhuan says: 'Do not for a tiger append wings; when it flies into a town it will choose people to devour.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [6]: The Outer Commentary to the Han Odes warns against giving a tiger wings.

sentences.zh: 」夫置不肖之人土位，是為虎傅翼也。

translations.literal: To place unworthy men on the earth's high seats is to append wings to a tiger.
translations.idiomatic: Seating unworthy men in high office was likened to that fatal gift.

sentences.zh: 注[七]春秋保干圖曰：「臣功大者主威侵，權並族害*

translations.literal: Note [7] The Chunqiu Baogan tu says: 'When a minister's achievement is too great, the ruler's awesome might is encroached upon; when power pools in one clan, kinsmen harm one another
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [7]: The apocryphal chart warns that overmighty ministers diminish the throne and that concentrated kin power turns clans against one another.

translations.literal: (corpse)
translations.idiomatic: The manuscript carries the gloss corpse at this break in the quotation.

sentences.zh: **[己]*奸行，吐珠於澤，誰能不含。

translations.literal: Wicked conduct follows; pearls are spat into the marsh—who can keep from closing his mouth on them.'
translations.idiomatic: The quotation continues with evil deeds and the image of pearls cast into a marsh, tempting every mouth to seize them.

sentences.zh: 」諭君之權柄外假，則必競取以為己利，猶珠出於澤中，誰能不含取以為己寶也。

translations.literal: It glosses that if the ruler's authority is lent abroad, all will struggle to seize it for private profit; it is like pearls issuing from a marsh—who can keep from holding them in the mouth as his treasure.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary explains that delegated power tempts every hand to seize private gain, like pearls lying in a fen that no mouth can resist pocketing.

sentences.zh: 吐猶出也。

translations.literal: 'Spit' means 'issue forth.'
translations.idiomatic: Here 'spit' means simply to cast forth.

sentences.zh: 注[八]老子道經曰：「魚不可脫於泉，國之利器不可以示人。

translations.literal: Note [8] Laozi's Dao jing says: 'Fish must not be taken from the deep; the state's sharp tools must not be shown to the people.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [8]: Laozi's Dao jing compares state secrets to fish that must stay in deep water.

sentences.zh: 」河上公注曰：「利器謂權道也。

translations.literal: Lord He of the River's commentary says: 'Sharp tools mean the arts of power.'
translations.idiomatic: Heshang Gong glosses sharp tools as techniques of rule.

sentences.zh: 理國權道，不可以示執事之臣。」

translations.literal: The arts that govern the state must not be displayed to ministers who hold office.'
translations.idiomatic: Those techniques must not be exposed to every petty official.

sentences.zh: 夫儉德之恭，政存約節。

translations.literal: Now frugality is the grace of virtue; government rests in restraint and moderation.
translations.idiomatic: Frugality is the beauty of virtue, and sound government lives in restraint.

sentences.zh: [一]故文帝愛百金於露台，飾帷帳於皁囊。

translations.literal: [1] Therefore Emperor Wen cherished the hundred jin of cash for a terrace and trimmed hanging curtains from black document pouches.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Emperor Wen had refused a terrace that cost a hundred jin of cash and had curtains sewn from memorial bags.

sentences.zh: [二]或有譏其儉者，上曰：「朕為天下守財耳，豈得妄用之哉！

translations.literal: [2] Some mocked his frugality; the emperor said, 'I am guarding the realm's wealth for All under Heaven—how dare I squander it wantonly!'
translations.idiomatic: [2] When mocked for stinginess, he replied that he was steward of the empire's purse, not its squanderer.

sentences.zh: 」至倉谷腐而不可食，錢貫朽而不可校。

translations.literal: In the end granary grain rotted until it could not be eaten, and strings of cash rotted until they could not be counted.
translations.idiomatic: His thrift left granaries bursting until grain spoiled and cash strings rotted uncounted.

sentences.zh: 今自初政已來，日月未久，費用賞賜已不可筭。

translations.literal: Now, from the beginning of Your Majesty's government, not many months have passed, yet outlays in fees and rewards are already past reckoning.
translations.idiomatic: Yet in the few months since the present reign began, gifts and stipends had already surpassed counting.

sentences.zh: 斂天下之財，積無功之家，帑藏單盡，民物雕傷，卒有不虞，復當重賦百姓，怨叛既生，危亂可待也。

translations.literal: You levy the wealth of the realm and pile it in houses that have done no service; the treasury is drained bare and the people and goods are wasted away—if a crisis suddenly comes, you will again impose heavy taxes on the hundred surnames; once resentment and rebellion arise, disaster and chaos will be close at hand.
translations.idiomatic: Taxing the realm to enrich idle mansions had emptied the treasury and beggared the people; another emergency would demand fresh levies, and hatred would soon breed revolt.

sentences.zh: 注[一]左氏傳魯大夫御孫曰「儉，德之恭；

translations.literal: Note [1] The Zuo Tradition: the grandee of Lu, Yu Sun, said, 'Frugality is the grace of virtue;
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Yu Sun of Lu praised thrift as the ornament of virtue—

sentences.zh: 侈，惡之大」也。

translations.literal: extravagance is the chief of evils.'
translations.idiomatic: and condemned extravagance as the root of vice.

sentences.zh: 注[二]文帝常欲作露台，計直百金。

translations.literal: Note [2] Emperor Wen once wished to build a viewing terrace; the estimate came to a hundred jin of cash.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Emperor Wen had priced a pleasure terrace at a hundred jin of metal.

sentences.zh: 曰：「百金中人十家之產，何以台為？

translations.literal: He said, 'A hundred jin is the substance of ten middle households—why build a terrace for that?'
translations.idiomatic: He said that a hundred jin equaled the entire means of ten middling families and asked what need there was of a terrace.

sentences.zh: 」遂止不作。

translations.literal: Thereupon he stopped and did not build it.
translations.idiomatic: So he abandoned the project.

sentences.zh: 又東方朔曰：「文帝集上書囊以為殿帷。」

translations.literal: Further, Dongfang Shuo said, 'Emperor Wen collected document bags from memorial submissions and used them as hall curtains.'
translations.idiomatic: Dongfang Shuo added that Wen had sewn court document pouches into hall drapes.

sentences.zh: 昔成王之政，周公在前，邵公在後，畢公在左，史佚在右，四子挾而維之。

translations.literal: In the government of King Cheng of old, the Duke of Zhou stood before him, the Duke of Shao behind him, the Duke of Bi on his left, and Scribe Yi on his right—the four men supported him on every side.
translations.idiomatic: King Cheng of Zhou had been ringed by the dukes of Zhou, Shao, and Bi and by Scribe Yi—four mentors bracing the throne.

sentences.zh: 目見正容，耳聞正言，一日即位，天下曠然，言其法度素定也。

translations.literal: His eyes saw correct bearing and his ears heard correct words; the day he took the throne the realm was at ease, meaning that measures and models had long been fixed.
translations.idiomatic: Correct models met his eyes and ears, so that when he mounted the throne the realm felt secure—policy was already settled.

sentences.zh: 今陛下有成王之尊而無數子之佐，雖欲崇雍熙，致太平，其可得乎？

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty has the dignity of a completed King Cheng yet lacks the assistance of several such men—though you desire to exalt perfect harmony and realize great peace, how can it be obtained?
translations.idiomatic: The throne now had King Cheng's dignity but not his ring of wise ministers; how could perfect peace follow from that?

sentences.zh: 自去年已來，□譴頻數，地坼天崩，高岸為谷。

translations.literal: Since last year, strange omens and reproofs have come in quick succession; the earth has split and heaven seemed to collapse, high banks became valleys.
translations.idiomatic: Since the previous year omens and reprimands had crowded in—earth fissures, heaven's portents, cliffs turned to gullies.

sentences.zh: 修身恐懼，則轉禍為福；

translations.literal: If you cultivate yourself in fear and trembling, you can turn misfortune into blessing;
translations.idiomatic: Earnest self-scrutiny could yet turn ill luck to good.

sentences.zh: 輕慢天戒，則其害彌深。

translations.literal: if you treat heaven's warnings lightly, the harm will go deeper still.
translations.idiomatic: Contempt for those warnings would deepen the wound.

sentences.zh: 願陛下親自勞恤，研精緻思，勉求忠貞之臣，誅遠佞諂之黨，損玉堂之盛，尊天爵之重，[一]割情慾之歡，罷宴私之好。

translations.literal: I pray Your Majesty will personally labor to comfort the people, refine your mind and ponder to the utmost, strive to seek loyal and steadfast ministers, execute and banish the faction of slick flatterers, reduce the splendor of the jade hall, honor the weight of heaven's nobility, [1] cut off the pleasures of passion and desire, and end personal banqueting favorites.
translations.idiomatic: He begged the emperor to toil for the people, think deeply, promote loyal men, purge flatterers, curb palace luxury, prize moral worth over display, bridle appetite, and end private revels.

sentences.zh: 帝王圖籍，陳列左右，心存亡國所以失之，鑒觀興王所以得之，庶□害可息，豐年可招矣。

translations.literal: Keep charts of emperors and kings arrayed to your left and right; let your mind dwell on how perished states lost them and take warning from how rising kings gained them—then perhaps harm can rest and abundant years can be summoned.
translations.idiomatic: Portraits of past rulers should line the halls as reminders of how states rise and fall—only then might harm cease and good harvests return.

sentences.zh: 注[一]孟子曰：「公卿大夫，人爵也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Mencius says: 'Dukes, ministers, and grandees are human nobility.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Mencius distinguishes human rank—

sentences.zh: 仁義禮智信，天爵也。」

translations.literal: Benevolence, right, ritual, wisdom, and trustworthiness are heaven's nobility.'
translations.idiomatic: from the heaven-given virtues of benevolence, right, ritual, wisdom, and trustworthiness.

sentences.zh: 書奏不省，而外戚寵臣鹹畏惡之。

translations.literal: The memorial was submitted but drew no response, while favored outer kin and courtiers all feared and detested him.
translations.idiomatic: The throne ignored the memorial, and the powerful in-laws and favorites hated Zhai Pu for it.

sentences.zh: 延光三年，出為酒泉太守。

translations.literal: In the third year of Yanguang he went out as grand administrator of Jiuquan.
translations.idiomatic: In the third year of the Yanguang era he was sent out as grand administrator of Jiuquan.

sentences.zh: 叛羌千餘騎徙敦煌來鈔郡界，酺赴擊，斬首九百級，羌觿幾盡，威名大震。

translations.literal: More than a thousand riders of rebel Qiang moved from Dunhuang to plunder the commandery border; Pu rushed to attack them, taking nine hundred heads; the Qiang dissidents were nearly exhausted, and his awesome name shook the region.
translations.idiomatic: When over a thousand rebel Qiang horsemen from Dunhuang raided the frontier, Zhai Pu counterattacked, claimed nine hundred heads, and all but destroyed the band; his reputation thundered across the northwest.

sentences.zh: 遷京兆尹。

translations.literal: He was transferred to metropolitan superintendent of Jingzhao.
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted to metropolitan superintendent of the capital region.

sentences.zh: 順帝即位，拜光祿大夫，遷將作大匠。

translations.literal: When Emperor Shun mounted the throne, he was appointed supernumerary grandee of the household and promoted superintendent of construction.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Shun appointed him supernumerary grandee of the Household and then superintendent of imperial works.

sentences.zh: 損省經用，歲息四五千萬。

translations.literal: He cut and saved on regular expenditures, each year saving forty or fifty million cash.
translations.idiomatic: By trimming routine outlays he saved forty or fifty million cash every year.

sentences.zh: [一]屢因□異，多所匡正。

translations.literal: [1] Repeatedly, responding to strange portents [text damaged], he corrected much.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Again and again he used anomalies as occasions to set policy straight, though one graph in the source is illegible.

sentences.zh: [三]由是權貴共誣酺及尚書令高堂芝等交通屬托，坐減死歸家。

translations.literal: [3] Thereby the powerful and noble together slandered Pu and Minister Secretariat Director Gao Tang Zhi and others for illicit connections and requests; he was sentenced to commuted death and returned home.
translations.idiomatic: [3] The great families jointly accused Zhai Pu and Secretariat Director Gao Tang Zhi of taking bribes and pulling strings; he escaped execution by one degree and was sent home.

sentences.zh: 復被章雲酺前與河南張楷等謀反，逮詣廷尉。

translations.literal: Again he was charged by memorial that Pu earlier had plotted rebellion with Zhang Kai of Henan and others; he was seized and sent to the commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: A fresh memorial claimed he had once conspired with Zhang Kai of Henan to rebel; he was arrested and remanded to the minister of justice.

sentences.zh: 及杜真等上書訟之，事得明釋。

translations.literal: When Du Zhen and others submitted memorials pleading his case, the affair was cleared and explained.
translations.idiomatic: Du Zhen and others filed counter-memorials, and the charge was exposed as false.

sentences.zh: 卒於家。

translations.literal: He died at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died in retirement at his house.
Endnote marker for commentary three.

sentences.zh: 注[一]經，常也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Jing means constant.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Here jing denotes the standing or regular budget.

sentences.zh: 注[二]益部耆舊傳曰：「時詔問酺陰陽失序，水旱隔並，其設銷復興濟之本。

translations.literal: Note [2] The Yibu qijiu zhuan says: 'At that time an edict asked Pu about yin and yang falling out of order, flood and drought coming together, and the root measures to dispel disaster and bring relief.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: The court asked Zhai Pu how to repair cosmic imbalance when flood and drought struck together.

sentences.zh: 酺上奏陳圖書之意曰：『漢四百年將有弱主閉門聽難之禍，數在三百年之閒。

translations.literal: Pu submitted a memorial stating the meaning of the charts and books, saying: 'Han at four hundred years will have the misfortune of a weak ruler who shuts the gate and listens to disaster; the reckoning falls within the space of three hundred years.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Pu answered with a memorial on the prognostications: the charts foretold that around the fourth century of Han a feeble emperor would bar his gates to calamity, with the fatal count falling near the three-hundred-year mark.

translations.literal: The manuscript carries a single asterisk as an editorial mark at this break in the quotation.
translations.idiomatic: An asterisk in the commentary marks an interruption in the quoted apocryphon.

sentences.zh: (宜升)

translations.literal: The gloss characters yi sheng appear in parentheses at this point in the manuscript.
translations.idiomatic: Parenthetical characters in the source suggest a textual emendation at this break.

sentences.zh: **[斗]*歷改憲，*[宜]*行先王至德要道，奉率時禁，抑損奢侈，宣明質樸，以延四百年之難。

translations.literal: **[Dou]* change the calendar constitution, *[it is fitting]* to practice the former kings' utmost virtue and essential way, honor the prohibitions of the season, check extravagance, make plain simplicity, and thereby postpone the disaster of the four hundred years.'
translations.idiomatic: The damaged quotation urges calendar reform around the dou pitch, return to the ancient kings' core virtues, seasonal sumptuary law, and plain living to push back the four-hundred-year crisis.

sentences.zh: 』帝從之。」

translations.literal: ' The emperor followed it.'"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted the advice, says the quotation.

sentences.zh: 注[三]益部耆舊傳曰：「杜真字孟宗，廣漢綿竹人也。

translations.literal: Note [3] The Yibu qijiu zhuan says: 'Du Zhen, courtesy name Mengzong, was a man of Mianzhu in Guanghan.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: The regional tradition introduces Du Zhen of Mianzhu, styled Mengzong.

sentences.zh: 少有孝行，習易、春秋，誦百萬言，兄事同郡翟酺。

translations.literal: In youth he had filial conduct, studied the Changes and the Chunqiu, and memorized a million graphs; he treated Zhai Pu of the same commandery as an elder brother.
translations.idiomatic: From boyhood he was known for filial piety, mastered the Changes and the Spring and Autumn, memorized a vast corpus, and honored Zhai Pu of his commandery as an elder brother.

sentences.zh: 酺後被系獄，真上檄章救酺，系獄笞六百，竟免酺難，京師莫不壯之。」

translations.literal: Later when Pu was imprisoned, Zhen submitted a written plea to save Pu; he was imprisoned and flogged six hundred strokes, yet in the end he freed Pu from disaster—none in the capital did not admire him.'"
translations.idiomatic: When Zhai Pu was jailed, Du Zhen filed a desperate appeal, took six hundred blows for it, and still won Pu's release; the capital applauded his courage.

sentences.zh: 著援神、鉤命解詁十二篇。

translations.literal: He wrote twelve chapters of commentary on the Yuanshen and Gouming.
translations.idiomatic: He composed twelve fascicles of glosses on the apocryphal Yuanshen and Gouming texts.
Endnote marker for commentary one.

sentences.zh: 注[一]援神契，鉤命決，皆孝經緯篇名也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Yuanshen qi and Gouming jue are both chapter titles among the Xiaojing weft texts.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Both titles belong to the weft-book tradition attached to the Classic of Filial Piety.

sentences.zh: 詁音古。

translations.literal: Gu is read like gu meaning 'explain.'
translations.idiomatic: The commentary glosses gu with the usual reading for exegesis.

sentences.zh: 初，酺之為大匠，上言：「孝文皇帝始置一經博士，[一]武帝大合天下之書，[二] 而孝宣論六經於石渠，學者滋盛，弟子萬數。

translations.literal: Earlier, when Pu was superintendent of construction, he submitted a statement: 'Emperor Wen first established erudites for a single classic; [1] Emperor Wu broadly gathered the books of All under Heaven; [2] while Emperor Xuan debated the six classics at the Stone Canal—scholars flourished and disciples numbered in the tens of thousands.'
translations.idiomatic: As superintendent, Zhai Pu had memorialized that Emperor Wen began with single-classic chairs, Emperor Wu gathered the empire's libraries, and Emperor Xuan debated six classics at Stone Canal, producing multitudes of students.

sentences.zh: [三]光武初興，愍其荒廢，起太學博士捨、內外講堂，諸生橫巷，為海內所集。

translations.literal: [3] When Guangwu first rose, pitying their ruin and waste, he raised the Grand Academy, erudite lodgings, inner and outer lecture halls, and lanes of students—it became the gathering place of the realm.'
translations.idiomatic: [3] Emperor Guangwu, grieving the ruin of learning, had rebuilt the Grand Academy with dormitories, inner and outer lecture halls, and student quarters that drew scholars from everywhere.

sentences.zh: 明帝時辟雍始成，欲毀太學，太尉趙□以為太學、辟雍皆宜兼存，故並傳至今。

translations.literal: In Emperor Ming's time the Biyong was completed; they wished to destroy the Grand Academy; Grand Commandant Zhao [lacuna] held that Grand Academy and Biyong should both be kept, and therefore both have been transmitted to the present.'
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Ming finished the circular moat school, some wanted the Grand Academy torn down; Grand Commandant Zhao, whose personal name is missing in the text, argued both institutions should survive, and both stand today.

sentences.zh: 而頃者頹廢，至為園采芻牧之處。

translations.literal: But recently it has decayed and wasted until it became a place for garden plots, gathering fodder, and herding.
translations.idiomatic: Lately the grounds had fallen into disuse as vegetable patches, hayfields, and sheep walks.

sentences.zh: 宜更修繕，誘進後學。

translations.literal: It is fitting to repair and build anew and entice later students.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Pu urged a fresh restoration to attract the next generation of learners.

sentences.zh: 」帝從之。

translations.literal: ' The emperor followed it.'
translations.idiomatic: The throne approved his plan.

sentences.zh: 酺免後，遂起太學，更開拓房室，學者為酺立碑銘於學雲。

translations.literal: After Pu was dismissed, thereupon they raised the Grand Academy, again opened and expanded rooms, and scholars set up a stele inscription for Pu at the academy, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: After his disgrace the Grand Academy was rebuilt and expanded; students are said to have raised a stele in his honor on the campus.

sentences.zh: 注[一]武帝建元五年始置五經博士，文帝之時未遑庠序之事，酺之此言，不知何據。

translations.literal: Note [1] In Emperor Wu's Jianyuan 5 the five-classics erudites were first established; in Emperor Wen's time there was not yet leisure for school matters—what Pu's statement relies on is unknown.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The Hou Han author notes that five-classics chairs began under Wu, not Wen, so Zhai Pu's dating is puzzling.

sentences.zh: 注[二]武帝詔曰：「其令禮官勸學，舉遺興禮。

translations.literal: Note [2] Emperor Wu's edict said: 'Let the ritual officials encourage learning and raise the lost and promote ritual.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Emperor Wu had ordered the ritual bureau to revive neglected learning and rites.

sentences.zh: 」舉遺謂搜求遺逸，是合天下之書也。

translations.literal: 'Raise the lost' means to search out the scattered and hidden—this is gathering the books of All under Heaven.'
translations.idiomatic: Raising the lost meant recovering lost texts, that is, the empire-wide book hunt.

sentences.zh: 注[三]宣帝甘露三年，詔諸儒講五經於殿中，兼平公羊、谷梁同異，上親臨決焉。

translations.literal: Note [3] In Emperor Xuan's Ganlu 3 an edict ordered the scholars to lecture on the five classics in the palace, also settling agreements and differences between Gongyang and Guliang, and the emperor personally attended to decide.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: In Ganlu 3 Emperor Xuan held a palace conference on the five classics and adjudicated Gongyang versus Guliang in person.

sentences.zh: 時更崇谷梁傳，故此言「六經」也。

translations.literal: At that time they further honored the Guliang commentary, hence this speaks of 'six classics.'
translations.idiomatic: Because Guliang was then elevated alongside the older five, contemporaries spoke of six classics.

sentences.zh: 石渠，閣名。

translations.literal: Stone Canal is the name of a pavilion.
translations.idiomatic: Stone Canal was the name of the palace gallery where the debate met.

sentences.zh: 昭帝時博士弟子員百人，宣帝末增倍之，元帝時詔無置弟子員，以廣學者，故言以萬數也。

translations.literal: In Emperor Zhao's time erudite disciples numbered one hundred; at the end of Emperor Xuan it was doubled; in Emperor Yuan's time an edict ordered not to fix a quota of disciples so as to broaden learners—hence it says they numbered in the tens of thousands.
translations.idiomatic: Student rolls had grown from one hundred under Zhao to doubled under Xuan, then Yuan removed caps so enrollments could soar toward ten thousand.

sentences.zh: 應奉字世叔，汝南南頓人也。

translations.literal: Ying Feng, courtesy name Shishu, was a man of Nandun in Runan.
translations.idiomatic: Ying Feng, styled Shishu, came from Nandun in Runan commandery.

sentences.zh: 曾祖父順，字華仲。

translations.literal: His great-grandfather Shun, courtesy name Huazhong.
translations.idiomatic: His great-grandfather Ying Shun bore the courtesy name Huazhong.

sentences.zh: 和帝時為河南尹、將作大匠，公廉約己，明達政事。

translations.literal: In Emperor He's time he was metropolitan governor of Henan and superintendent of construction, fair and incorrupt, restraining himself, clear and penetrating in government affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor He he served as metropolitan governor of Henan and superintendent of works, famed for clean self-discipline and shrewd administration.

sentences.zh: [一]生十子，皆有才學。

translations.literal: [1] He fathered ten sons, all possessing talent and learning.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He had ten sons, each accomplished as a scholar.

sentences.zh: 中子疊，江夏太守。

translations.literal: The middle son Die was grand administrator of Jiangxia.
translations.idiomatic: His third son Die became grand administrator of Jiangxia.

sentences.zh: 疊生郴，武陵太守。

translations.literal: Die fathered Chen; Chen was grand administrator of Wuling.
translations.idiomatic: Die begat Chen, who in turn governed Wuling as grand administrator.

sentences.zh: 郴生奉。

translations.literal: Chen fathered Feng.
translations.idiomatic: Chen was the father of Ying Feng.

sentences.zh: 注[一]華嶠書曰：「華仲少給事郡縣，為吏清公，不發私書。

translations.literal: Note [1] Hua Qiao's book says: 'Huazhong in youth served the commandery and county as clerk, was a fair official, and did not open private letters.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Hua Qiao describes young Shun as a scrupulous clerk who never opened private mail.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉，尚書郎轉右丞，遷冀州刺史，廉直無私。

translations.literal: Recommended filial and incorrupt, he was gentleman of the palace, transferred to right aide, promoted regional inspector of Ji—incorrupt and straight without private bias.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended as filial and incorrupt, he rose through palace gentleman and right aide to regional inspector of Ji, unstained by favoritism.

sentences.zh: 遷東平相，賞罰必信，吏不敢犯。

translations.literal: He was promoted chancellor of Dongping; rewards and punishments were always kept—clerks did not dare offend.
translations.idiomatic: As chancellor of Dongping he enforced rewards and punishments so consistently that clerks feared to transgress.

sentences.zh: 有梓樹生於廳事室上，事後母至孝，觿以為孝感之應。

translations.literal: A catalpa tree grew atop the chamber of his headquarters building; because he served his stepmother with extreme filial piety, some took it as a response of sympathetic resonance.
translations.idiomatic: A catalpa sprouted on his courtroom roof; observers attributed it to his extreme filial care for his stepmother.

sentences.zh: 時竇憲出屯河西，刺史、二千石皆遣子弟奉賂遺憲，憲敗後鹹被繩黜，順獨不在其中，由是顯名。

translations.literal: At that time Dou Xian camped west of the river; regional inspectors and two-thousand-bushel officials all sent sons and younger brothers to bring bribes and gifts to Xian; after Xian's defeat all were impeached and dismissed—only Shun was not among them, whereby his name became illustrious.
translations.idiomatic: When Dou Xian held the western frontier, local magnates sent sons with bribes; after Xian's fall they were purged, but Shun alone had stayed clean and so won renown.

sentences.zh: 為將作大匠，視事五年，省費億萬。

translations.literal: As superintendent of construction he handled affairs five years and saved expenses in the hundreds of millions.
translations.idiomatic: Five years as superintendent of works yielded savings reckoned in the hundreds of millions.

sentences.zh: 」汝南記曰「華仲妻本是汝南鄧元義前妻也。

translations.literal: ' The Record of Runan says: 'Huazhong's wife had originally been the former wife of Deng Yuanyi of Runan.'"
translations.idiomatic: The Runan gazetteer adds that Shun's wife had been Deng Yuanyi's divorced first wife.

sentences.zh: 元義父伯考為尚書僕射，元義還鄉里，妻留事姑甚謹，姑憎之，幽閉空室，節其食飲，羸露日困，妻終無怨言。

translations.literal: Yuanyi's father Bokao was minister director of secretaries; Yuanyi returned to his village, while the wife remained to serve her mother-in-law with great care; the mother-in-law hated her, shut her in an empty room, restricted her food and drink, and she grew emaciated and daily weaker, yet the wife never uttered resentment.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Yuanyi's father Bokao was vice-director of the Secretariat; Yuanyi went home, but his wife stayed to wait on her mother-in-law until the old woman, despising her, locked her up and starved her—still she never complained.

sentences.zh: 後伯考怪而問之。

translations.literal: Later Bokao wondered and asked about it.
translations.idiomatic: When Bokao noticed her state, he pressed for an explanation.

sentences.zh: 時義子朗年數歲，言母不病，但苦饑耳。

translations.literal: At that time Yuanyi's son Lang was several years old and said, 'Mother is not ill—she only suffers hunger.'
translations.idiomatic: Yuanyi's little boy Lang said plainly that his mother was not sick, only starving.

sentences.zh: 伯考流涕曰：『何意親姑反為此禍！

translations.literal: Bokao wept and said, 'How could a dear aunt by marriage bring such a disaster!'
translations.idiomatic: Bokao burst into tears at the cruelty of a kinswoman-by-marriage.

sentences.zh: 』因遣歸家。

translations.literal: ' Thereupon he sent her back to her parents' home.'
translations.idiomatic: He sent the daughter-in-law home to her own family at once.

sentences.zh: 更嫁為華仲妻。

translations.literal: She married again and became Huazhong's wife.
translations.idiomatic: She later remarried Ying Shun and became Huazhong's consort.

sentences.zh: 仲為將作大匠，妻乘朝車出，元義於路傍觀之，謂人曰：『此我故婦，非有它過，家夫人遇之實酷，本自相貴。

translations.literal: Huazhong being superintendent of construction, his wife rode out in the court carriage; Yuanyi watched from the roadside and told people, 'That is my former wife—she had no other fault; the old lady at home treated her with real cruelty; she was always noble in herself.'
translations.idiomatic: One day Shun's wife rode the official carriage past Yuanyi, who told bystanders that she had been his blameless ex-wife ruined by his mother's cruelty.

sentences.zh: 』其子朗時為郎，母與書皆不荅，與衣裳輒燒之。

translations.literal: ' Her son Lang then held the post of gentleman attendant; the mother sent him letters and he never answered; when she sent clothes he burned them.'
translations.idiomatic: Their son Lang, now a court gentleman, refused her letters and burned the clothes she sent.

sentences.zh: 母不以介意，意欲見之，乃至親家李氏堂上，令人以它詞請朗。

translations.literal: The mother did not take it to heart and still wished to see him; she therefore went to the hall of her in-laws the Li family and had someone invite Lang with another excuse.
translations.idiomatic: Undeterred, she contrived a meeting at her Li in-laws' house under a false pretext.

sentences.zh: 朗至，見母，再拜涕泣，因起出。

translations.literal: When Lang arrived and saw his mother, he bowed twice, wept, then rose and went out.
translations.idiomatic: Lang came, bowed to his mother in tears, and fled the room.

sentences.zh: 母追謂之曰：『我幾死，自為汝家所□，我何罪過，乃如此邪？

translations.literal: The mother chased him and said, 'I nearly died and was [lacuna] by your family—what crime of mine deserved such treatment?'
translations.idiomatic: She ran after him crying that she had nearly perished at their hands through no fault of her own.

sentences.zh: 』因此遂絕」也。

translations.literal: ' From this they broke off relations completely.'"
translations.idiomatic: After that mother and son never spoke again.

sentences.zh: 奉少聰明，自為童兒及長，凡所經履，莫不暗記。

translations.literal: Feng from youth was intelligent; from the time he was a child until grown, whatever he passed through he never failed to remember in secret.
translations.idiomatic: Ying Feng had been prodigious from childhood: every journey and sight fixed itself in his memory without effort.

sentences.zh: 讀書五行並下。

translations.literal: He read five lines at a time.
translations.idiomatic: He could take in five columns of text at a single glance.

sentences.zh: 為郡決曹史，行部四十二縣，錄囚徒數百千人。

translations.literal: He was bureau clerk for decisions in the commandery; when touring the forty-two subordinate counties he recorded prisoners numbering in the hundreds and thousands.
translations.idiomatic: As the commandery's sentencing clerk he inspected forty-two counties and logged hundreds or thousands of inmates.

sentences.zh: 及還，太守備問之，奉口說罪系姓名，坐狀輕重，無所遺脫，時人奇之。

translations.literal: When he returned, the grand administrator questioned him in full; Feng orally stated the prisoners' names, crimes, and degrees of guilt without omitting a single one—people of the time marveled at him.
