translations.idiomatic: Back at headquarters he recited every prisoner's name, charge, and sentence from memory and astonished his superiors.

sentences.zh: [一]著漢書後序，多所述載。

translations.literal: [1] He wrote a sequel to the Han shu with much recorded therein.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He composed a long sequel to the Book of Han preserving many details.

sentences.zh: [二]大將軍梁冀舉茂才。

translations.literal: [2] Grand General Liang Ju recommended him as flourishing talent.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Grand General Liang Ju nominated him as flourishing talent.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承書曰：「奉少為上計吏，許訓為計掾，俱到京師。

translations.literal: Note [1] Xie Cheng's book says: 'Feng in youth was chief clerk for the annual accounting; Xu Xun was accounting aide; they both reached the capital.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Xie Cheng tells how young Feng went to the capital as chief clerk on the annual accounting mission beside Xu Xun.

sentences.zh: 訓自發鄉里，在路晝頓暮宿，所見長吏、賓客、亭長、吏卒、奴僕，訓皆密疏姓名，欲試奉。

translations.literal: Xun from the time he set out from his village, on the road halting by day and lodging by night, secretly wrote down the names of every senior official, guest, station chief, clerk, servant, and slave he saw, wishing to test Feng.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Xun secretly listed every official, guest, post-house clerk, and servant they met along the road to test Feng's memory.

sentences.zh: 還郡，出疏示奉。

translations.literal: When they returned to the commandery, he produced the list and showed it to Feng.
translations.idiomatic: On returning home he handed Feng his written tally.

sentences.zh: 奉云：『前食穎川綸氏都亭，亭長胡奴名祿，以飲漿來，何不在疏？

translations.literal: Feng said: 'Earlier when I ate at the Guan prefectural pavilion in Yingchuan, the Hu slave of the station chief, named Lu, who brought the drink—why is he not on the list?'
translations.idiomatic: Feng immediately spotted one omission: a station chief's Hu slave named Lu who had served him drink in Yingchuan.

sentences.zh: 』坐中皆驚。

translations.literal: ' All seated were astonished.'
translations.idiomatic: Everyone present was dumbfounded.

sentences.zh: 」又云：「奉年二十時，嘗詣彭城相袁賀，賀時出行閉門，造車匠於內開扇出半面視奉，奉即委去。

translations.literal: It also says: 'When Feng was twenty sui he once visited Chancellor Yuan He of Pengcheng; He was out and the gate was closed; a cart maker inside opened the door halfway and looked at Feng with half his face; Feng thereupon turned away and left.'
translations.idiomatic: The same source tells how at twenty he called on Yuan He of Pengcheng, saw only a wheelwright's half face through a cracked door, and walked away.

sentences.zh: 後數十年於路見車匠，識而呼之。」

translations.literal: Decades later on the road he saw the cart maker, recognized him, and called out to him.'"
translations.idiomatic: Years later he recognized that same wheelwright on the road and hailed him by sight.

sentences.zh: 注[二]袁山松書曰：「奉又刪史記、漢書及漢記三百六十餘年，自漢興至其時，凡十七卷，名曰漢事。」

translations.literal: Note [2] Yuan Shansong's book says: 'Feng also abridged the Shiji, the Han shu, and the Han ji for more than three hundred sixty years, from Han's rise down to his own time, in seventeen fascicles, entitled Han Affairs.'"
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Yuan Shansong credits him with a seventeen-fascicle digest of Sima Qian, the Han histories, and the Han ji from the founding down to his day.

sentences.zh: 先是，武陵蠻詹山等四千餘人反叛，執縣令，屯結連年。

translations.literal: Earlier, the Wuling Man Zhanshan and others, more than four thousand, rebelled, seized the county magistrate, and encamped in linked camps for years.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier the Wuling tribesman Zhanshan had led four thousand rebels in seizing a magistrate and holding out for years.

sentences.zh: 詔下公卿議，四府舉奉才堪將帥。

translations.literal: An edict went down ordering the ministers to deliberate; the four high offices jointly recommended Feng as a man whose talent fitted command of armies.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked the high ministers for a plan, and the four offices nominated Ying Feng as the general the crisis needed.

sentences.zh: [一]永興元年，拜武陵太守。

translations.literal: [1] In the first year of Yongxing he was appointed grand administrator of Wuling.
translations.idiomatic: [1] In Yongxing 1 he became grand administrator of Wuling.

sentences.zh: 到官慰納，山等皆悉降散。

translations.literal: When he arrived at his post he comforted and gathered them in; the mountain leaders all submitted and dispersed.
translations.idiomatic: On arrival he soothed the tribes; Zhanshan and his followers surrendered and broke camp.

sentences.zh: 於是興學校，舉仄陋，政稱變俗。

translations.literal: Thereupon he raised schools, raised the obscure and lowly, and his administration was praised for changing customs.
translations.idiomatic: He founded schools, promoted men of mean origin, and his rule was praised for transforming local ways.

sentences.zh: 坐公事免。

translations.literal: He was dismissed for a public-office offense.
translations.idiomatic: He lost office over a procedural fault in public business.

sentences.zh: 注[一]四府，解見皇后紀。

translations.literal: Note [1] The four offices—see the empress's annals.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The four high offices are explained in the empress's basic annals.

sentences.zh: 延熹中，武陵蠻復寇亂荊州，車騎將軍馮緄以奉有威恩，為蠻夷所服，上請與俱征。

translations.literal: In the Xianxi years the Wuling Man again raided and threw Jingzhou into chaos; chariot-and-horse general Feng Gun, because Feng had awesome favor and kindness and was trusted by the Man and Yi, requested that Feng go on campaign with him.
translations.idiomatic: During Xianxi the Wuling tribes again ravaged Jingzhou; General of Chariots and Cavalry Feng Gun, knowing Ying Feng's prestige among the tribes, asked him to join the expedition.

sentences.zh: 拜從事中郎。

translations.literal: He was appointed staff supervisor.
translations.idiomatic: He received appointment as staff supervisor on the campaign.

sentences.zh: [一]奉勤設方略，賊破軍罷，緄推功於奉，薦為司隸校尉。

translations.literal: [1] Feng diligently devised strategy; when the bandits were broken and the army stood down, Gun pushed the merit to Feng and recommended him as metropolitan commandant.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Ying Feng planned the campaign; when the enemy broke, Gun credited him and recommended him as metropolitan commandant.

sentences.zh: 糾舉奸違，不避豪戚，以嚴厲為名。

translations.literal: He impeached wicked violations and did not avoid powerful kin, taking severity as his reputation.
translations.idiomatic: As metropolitan commandant he indicted wrongdoing without sparing great houses, earning a name for harsh justice.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承書曰：「時詔奉曰：『蠻夷叛逆作難，積惡放恣，鑊中之魚，火熾湯盡，當悉燋爛，以刷國恥。

translations.literal: Note [1] Xie Cheng's book says: 'At that time an edict told Feng: 'The Man and Yi rebel and make trouble, long accumulating evil and giving free rein; they are fish in the cauldron—fire blazes and the soup will boil dry; they ought all to be charred and done, to wipe away the state's shame.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Xie Cheng preserves an edict branding the rebels fish in a boiling pot who must be scorched away to cleanse national shame.

sentences.zh: 朝廷以奉昔守南土，威名播越，故複式序重任。

translations.literal: The court, because Feng once guarded the southern lands and his awesome name spread far, therefore again ordered him to this weighty charge.
translations.idiomatic: Because he had once ruled the south and his name still carried there, the court gave him the mission again.

sentences.zh: 奉之廢興，期在於今。

translations.literal: Whether Feng rises or falls hinges on this moment.
translations.idiomatic: His career now hung on this campaign.

sentences.zh: 賜奉錢十萬，駁犀方具□、金錯把刀□、革帶各一。

translations.literal: Grant Feng one hundred thousand cash, one set of rhinoceros-horn fittings (names unclear in the text), one gold-inlaid-hilt saber (name unclear in the text), and one each of leather belt.'
translations.idiomatic: The edict granted him one hundred thousand cash, rhinoceros-horn gear, a gold-inlaid saber, and a leather belt, though the manuscript is damaged on two gift names.

sentences.zh: 奉其勉之！』」

translations.literal: Let Feng exert himself!'"
translations.idiomatic: The edict closed by urging him to spare no effort!

sentences.zh: 及鄧皇后敗，而田貴人見幸，桓帝有建立之議。

translations.literal: When Empress Deng fell, the Honorable Lady Tian won favor, and Emperor Huan had a proposal to establish a new empress.
translations.idiomatic: After Empress Deng's fall, Lady Tian won Emperor Huan's favor, and he considered raising her as empress.

sentences.zh: 奉以田氏微賤，不宜超登後位，上書諫曰：「臣聞周納狄女，襄王出居於鄭；

translations.literal: Feng, holding that the Tian clan was mean and lowly and not fit to leap to empress's rank, submitted a memorial remonstrating, saying: 'I have heard that Zhou took a Di woman and King Xiang went out to dwell at Zheng;
translations.idiomatic: Ying Feng argued that the Tians were too humble for the throne of the consort, citing how Zhou took a Di bride and King Xiang had to flee to Zheng.

sentences.zh: [一]漢立飛燕，成帝胤嗣泯絕。

translations.literal: [1] Han raised Feiyan and Emperor Cheng's posterity was cut off.'
translations.idiomatic: [1] He reminded the throne that Emperor Cheng's favor for Flying Swallow Zhao ended his line.

sentences.zh: 母后之重，興廢所因。

translations.literal: The weight of empress dowager and empress is what rise and ruin depend on.
translations.idiomatic: The choice of empress governs whether the house rises or falls.

sentences.zh: 宜思關睢之所求，遠五禁之所忌。

translations.literal: It is fitting to ponder what the Guanju ode sought and to keep far from what the five prohibitions forbid.'
translations.idiomatic: He urged the emperor to seek a bride like the Guanju poem praises and to shun the five forbidden matches.

sentences.zh: 」[二]帝納其言，竟立竇皇后。

translations.literal: ' [2] The emperor accepted his words and in the end established Empress Dou.'
translations.idiomatic: [2] The emperor took the advice and enthroned Empress Dou instead.

sentences.zh: 注[一]左傳襄王將以狄女為後，富*

translations.literal: Note [1] The Zuo Tradition: King Xiang was about to take a Di woman as empress; Fu [lacuna]
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The Zuo cites King Xiang's plan to wed a Di princess—Fu Chen's name is damaged in the manuscript.

translations.literal: (chen)
translations.idiomatic: The gloss supplies the character chen for the damaged minister's name.

sentences.zh: **[辰]*諫曰：「不可，狄固貪惏，王又啟之。

translations.literal: **[Chen]* remonstrated, saying: 'You cannot—the Di are by nature greedy and cruel, and the king again opens the way for them.'
translations.idiomatic: The minister warned that the northern Di were rapacious and that the king courted disaster by inviting them.

sentences.zh: 」王不從。

translations.literal: ' The king did not follow.'
translations.idiomatic: King Xiang ignored the warning.

sentences.zh: 狄人伐周，襄王出奔。

translations.literal: The Di attacked Zhou; King Xiang fled into exile.
translations.idiomatic: Di tribes struck the royal domain and drove King Xiang from his capital.

sentences.zh: 注[二]韓詩外傳曰：「婦人有五不娶：喪婦之長女不娶，為其不受命也；

translations.literal: Note [2] The Han shi waizhuan says: 'There are five matches a man must not take: the eldest daughter of a widow—do not take her, because she does not accept Heaven's command;
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: The Outer Han Odes lists five forbidden brides, beginning with a widow's eldest daughter.

sentences.zh: 世有惡疾不娶，□於天也；

translations.literal: a family with foul disease—do not take her, [lacuna] against Heaven;
translations.idiomatic: A household afflicted with foul hereditary disease was another barred match.

sentences.zh: 世有刑人不娶，□於人也；

translations.literal: a family with mutilated convicts—do not take her, [lacuna] against other men;
translations.idiomatic: Families whose members bore mutilating punishments were likewise excluded.

sentences.zh: 亂家女不娶，類不正也；

translations.literal: a daughter from a disorderly house—do not take her, for the type is not upright;
translations.idiomatic: Women from morally chaotic clans were unsuitable.

sentences.zh: 逆家子不娶，廢人倫也。」

translations.literal: a son from a rebellious house—do not take him, for it overturns human relations.'"
translations.idiomatic: Sons of traitorous houses completed the list of forbidden unions.

sentences.zh: 及黨事起，奉乃慨然以疾自退。

translations.literal: When the partisan prosecutions arose, Feng thereupon sighed and withdrew on grounds of illness.
translations.idiomatic: When the faction cases erupted, Ying Feng retired in disgust, pleading sickness.

sentences.zh: 追愍屈原，因以自傷，著感騷三十篇，數萬言。

translations.literal: He mourned Qu Yuan and thereby wounded himself, composing thirty pieces of Gan Sao, several tens of thousands of graphs.
translations.idiomatic: He wrote thirty laments modeled on Encountering Sorrow, grieving Qu Yuan and his own plight.

sentences.zh: 諸公多薦舉，會病卒。

translations.literal: Many grandees recommended him, but he happened to die of illness.
translations.idiomatic: Notables repeatedly nominated him, but he died before another posting came.

sentences.zh: 子劭。

translations.literal: His son was Shao.
translations.idiomatic: His son was Ying Shao.

sentences.zh: 劭字仲遠。

translations.literal: Shao's courtesy name was Zhongyuan.
translations.idiomatic: Ying Shao took the courtesy name Zhongyuan.

sentences.zh: [一]少篤學，博覽多聞。

translations.literal: [1] In youth he was devoted to learning, broadly read and widely heard.
translations.idiomatic: From boyhood he studied deeply and read omnivorously.

sentences.zh: 靈帝時舉孝廉，辟車騎將軍何苗掾。

translations.literal: In Emperor Ling's time he was recommended filial and incorrupt and summoned as aide to General of Chariots and Cavalry He Miao.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Ling he took the filial-and-incorrupt examination and became an aide to General He Miao.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承書、*

translations.literal: Note [1] Xie Cheng's book and [lacuna]
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Xie Cheng's work and other sources diverge on detail.

translations.literal: (yue)
translations.idiomatic: The gloss character yue appears in parentheses in the manuscript.

sentences.zh: *應氏譜並云「字仲遠」，續漢書文士傳作「仲援」，漢官儀又作「*[仲]*瑗」，未知孰是。

translations.literal: *The Ying clan genealogy and other texts all say his courtesy name was Zhongyuan; the Xu Han shu Literary Men's Tradition writes Zhongyuan; the Han guan yi again writes Zhongyuan—which is correct is unknown.'
translations.idiomatic: Family registers give one form of his style, the Later Han literary tradition another, and the Han official precedents a third; editors cannot decide which is authentic.

sentences.zh: 中平二年，漢陽賊邊章、韓遂與羌胡為寇，東侵三輔，時遣車騎將軍皇甫嵩西討之。

translations.literal: In the second year of Zhongping, bandits Bian Zhang and Han Sui of Hanyang joined Qiang and Hu in raiding, invading east into the three adjuncts; at that time chariot-and-horse general Huangfu Song was sent west to punish them.
translations.idiomatic: In Zhongping 2 the Hanyang rebels Bian Zhang and Han Sui allied with Qiang and Hu, pushed east into the capital region, and Huangfu Song marched west against them.

sentences.zh: 嵩請發烏桓三千人。

translations.literal: Song requested to mobilize three thousand Wuhuan.
translations.idiomatic: Huangfu Song asked for three thousand Wuhuan auxiliaries.

sentences.zh: 北軍中候鄒靖上言：「烏桓觿弱，宜開募鮮卑。

translations.literal: Northern Army middle commander Zou Jing submitted a statement: 'The Wuhuan host is weak; it is fitting to open recruitment among the Xianbei.'
translations.idiomatic: Zou Jing argued the Wuhuan contingent was too small and proposed hiring Xianbei light horse instead.

sentences.zh: 」事下四府，大將軍掾韓卓議，以為「烏桓兵寡，而與鮮卑世為仇敵，若烏桓被發，則鮮卑必襲其家。

translations.literal: ' The matter went down to the four offices; chief clerk to the grand general Han Zhuo deliberated, holding that 'Wuhuan troops are few, yet with the Xianbei they are hereditary foes—if the Wuhuan are stripped bare, the Xianbei will surely raid their homes.'
translations.idiomatic: Han Zhuo warned that stripping Wuhuan garrisons would invite Xianbei raids on their families.

sentences.zh: 烏桓聞之，當復□軍還救。

translations.literal: When the Wuhuan hear it, they will again [lacuna] the army to return and save them.
translations.idiomatic: The Wuhuan would rush home to defend their hearths, crippling the campaign.

sentences.zh: 非唯無益於實，乃更沮三軍之情。

translations.literal: Not only would there be no benefit in fact—it would further damp the three armies' morale.
translations.idiomatic: The scheme would help neither the war effort nor the troops' spirit.

sentences.zh: 鄒靖居近邊塞，究其態詐。

translations.literal: Zou Jing dwells near the border ramparts and has investigated their deceitful ways.
translations.idiomatic: Zou Jing lived on the frontier and knew nomad duplicity firsthand.

sentences.zh: 若令靖募鮮卑輕騎五千，必有破敵之效」。

translations.literal: If you order Jing to recruit five thousand Xianbei light horse, there will surely be an effect of breaking the enemy.'"
translations.idiomatic: He insisted five thousand Xianbei riders under Zou Jing would shatter the rebels.

sentences.zh: 劭駁之曰：

translations.literal: Shao refuted it, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Ying Shao answered with a counter-memorial.

sentences.zh: 鮮卑隔在漠北，犬羊為腢，無君長之帥，廬落之居，而天性貪暴，不拘信義，故數犯障塞，且無寧歲。

translations.literal: The Xianbei are cut off beyond the desert north; they are flocks like dogs and sheep, without chieftains to lead them or settled camps to dwell in, yet by nature greedy and violent, unrestrained by faith or duty—therefore they repeatedly violate the barrier passes and never have a peaceful year.
translations.idiomatic: He painted the Xianbei as rootless nomads beyond the Gobi, faithless plunderers who yearly raided the frontier.

sentences.zh: 唯至互巿，乃來靡服。

translations.literal: Only when they reach the mutual markets do they come and submit softly.
translations.idiomatic: They feigned submission only at border markets when Chinese goods tempted them.

sentences.zh: 苟欲中國珍貨，非為畏威懷德。

translations.literal: If the court desires China's precious goods, it is not because they fear awe or cherish virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Their trade was greed for silk and metal, not respect for Han might.

sentences.zh: 計獲事足，旋踵為害。

translations.literal: Once their calculations gain enough, they wheel about and become a scourge.
translations.idiomatic: Sated with profit they always turned on their hosts.

sentences.zh: 是以朝家外而不內，蓋為此也。

translations.literal: Therefore the court treats them as outsiders and not insiders—largely for this reason.
translations.idiomatic: That is why wise policy kept them outside the pale.

sentences.zh: [一]往者匈奴反叛，度遼將軍馬續、烏桓校尉王元發鮮卑五千餘騎，又武威太守趙沖亦率鮮卑征討叛羌。

translations.literal: [1] Formerly when the Xiongnu rebelled, General Who Crosses the Liao Ma Xu and colonel of the Wuhuan Wang Yuan mobilized more than five thousand Xianbei riders, and further grand administrator of Wuwei Zhao Chong also led Xianbei to campaign against rebel Qiang.
translations.idiomatic: He cited Ma Xu and Wang Yuan hiring Xianbei against Xiongnu and Zhao Chong doing the same against Qiang—precedents that proved costly.

sentences.zh: 斬獲丑虜，既不足言，而鮮卑越溢，多為不法。

translations.literal: They beheaded captives hardly worth mentioning, yet the Xianbei overstepped bounds and mostly acted unlawfully.
translations.idiomatic: Those allies had taken heads yet mostly ran amok.

sentences.zh: 裁以軍令，則忿戾作亂；

translations.literal: If you restrain them with military law, they grow resentful and rebel;
translations.idiomatic: Strict discipline made them mutinous.

sentences.zh: 制御小緩，則陸掠殘害。

translations.literal: if control is slightly relaxed, they pillage and ravage on land.
translations.idiomatic: Loose rein let them loot settlements.

sentences.zh: 劫居人，鈔商旅，噉人牛羊，略人兵馬。

translations.literal: They rob settled people, plunder merchants, devour people's cattle and sheep, and seize people's horses and arms.
translations.idiomatic: They robbed farmers, waylaid caravans, rustled stock, and stole weapons.

sentences.zh: 得賞既多，不肯去，復欲以物買鐵。

translations.literal: Having gained rich rewards, they refuse to leave and further wish to use goods to buy iron.
translations.idiomatic: Paid off in silk, they stayed and demanded iron for more arms.

sentences.zh: 邊將不聽，便取縑帛聚欲燒之。

translations.literal: The border generals refused; they then gathered bolts of silk and threatened to burn them.
translations.idiomatic: When refused iron they piled silk to burn, blackmailing the commanders.

sentences.zh: 邊將恐怖，畏其反叛，辭謝撫順，無敢拒違。

translations.literal: The border generals were terrified, fearing they would rebel, apologized and soothed them, and none dared refuse.
translations.idiomatic: Frontier officers, fearing mutiny, bought peace with concessions.

sentences.zh: 今狡寇未殄，而羌為巨害，如或致悔，其可追乎！

translations.literal: Now the crafty bandits are not yet exterminated, yet the Qiang are a great scourge—if regret should follow, can it be pursued!
translations.idiomatic: With rebels still in the field, trusting Xianbei courted irreparable regret.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為可募隴西羌胡守善不叛者，簡其精勇，多其牢賞。

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly thinks you may recruit Qiang and Hu of Longxi who keep good faith and do not rebel, pick out their elite courage, and increase their rations and rewards.
translations.idiomatic: He proposed recruiting loyal Longxi Qiang auxiliaries, paying them well.

sentences.zh: [二]太守李參沈靜有謀，必能□厲得其死力。

translations.literal: [2] Grand Administrator Li Can is calm and resourceful and surely can [lacuna] rouse them to fight to the death.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Li Can of the commandery, steady and shrewd, could inspire such allies.

sentences.zh: 當思漸消之略，不可倉卒望也。

translations.literal: One ought to think of a plan to dissolve them gradually; one cannot hope for success in haste.
translations.idiomatic: Victory required a gradual strategy, not a rushed gamble.

sentences.zh: 韓卓復與劭相難反覆。

translations.literal: Han Zhuo again debated back and forth with Shao.
translations.idiomatic: Han Zhuo and Ying Shao traded further memorials.

sentences.zh: 於是詔百官大會朝堂，皆從劭議。

translations.literal: Thereupon an edict ordered a great assembly of the hundred officials in the court hall; all followed Shao's opinion.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor convened the ministers and adopted Ying Shao's view.

sentences.zh: 注[一]朝家猶國家也。

translations.literal: Note [1] 'Court house' means the same as the state.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: 'Court house' here means the central government.

sentences.zh: 公羊傳曰「春秋內諸夏而外夷狄」也。

translations.literal: The Gongyang says: 'The Chunqiu treats the Hua Xia as inner and the Yi Di as outer.'"
translations.idiomatic: The Gongyang contrasts inner Chinese states with outer barbarians.

sentences.zh: 注[二]牢，稟食也。

translations.literal: Note [2] Lao means rationed grain.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Lao here means issued rations.

sentences.zh: 或作「勞」。

translations.literal: Some manuscripts write 'lao' meaning merit.
translations.idiomatic: Some texts read the graph as merit instead of rations.

sentences.zh: 勞，功也。

translations.literal: Lao means achievement.
translations.idiomatic: In that reading it means reward for merit.

sentences.zh: 三年，舉高第，再遷，六年，拜太山太守。

translations.literal: In the third year he was recommended of highest rank, promoted twice; in the sixth year he was appointed grand administrator of Mount Tai.
translations.idiomatic: He ranked top in a third-year evaluation, rose twice, and in year six became grand administrator of Taishan.

sentences.zh: 初平二年，黃巾三十萬觿入郡界。

translations.literal: In the second year of Chuping, three hundred thousand Yellow Turbans in dissident bands entered the commandery border.
translations.idiomatic: Chuping 2 brought three hundred thousand Yellow Turbans swarming into Taishan.

sentences.zh: 劭糾率文武連與賊戰，前後斬首數千級，獲生口老弱萬餘人，輜重二千兩，賊皆退□，郡內以安。

translations.literal: Shao marshaled civil and military officers and repeatedly fought the bandits; in succession they took several thousand heads, captured more than ten thousand living persons old and weak, and two thousand baggage carts; the bandits all withdrew [lacuna], and the commandery was thereby secure.
translations.idiomatic: Ying Shao rallied his staff, killed thousands, took ten thousand prisoners and two thousand supply wagons, and drove the rebels from the commandery.

sentences.zh: 初，安帝時河閒人尹次﹑穎川人史玉皆坐殺人當死，次兄初及玉母軍並詣官曹求代其命，因縊而物故。

translations.literal: Earlier, in Emperor An's time, Yin Ci of Hejian and Shi Yu of Yingchuan both sat convicted of homicide and should die; Ci's elder brother Chu and Yu's mother Jun both went to the authorities seeking to substitute their lives, and thereupon hanged themselves and died.
translations.idiomatic: He recalled how Yin Ci and Shi Yu faced death for murder until kin offered to die in their place.

sentences.zh: 尚書陳忠以罪疑從輕，議活次﹑玉。

translations.literal: Secretariat Director Chen Zhong, because the crime was doubtful, treated it leniently and debated sparing Ci and Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Zhong had argued for mercy on grounds of doubt.

sentences.zh: 劭後追駁之，據正典刑，有可存者。

translations.literal: Shao later refuted him, holding to the correct statutes and punishments, yet with points that could be retained.
translations.idiomatic: Ying Shao later answered Chen Zhong, insisting on the letter of the law while conceding narrow exceptions.

sentences.zh: 其議曰：

translations.literal: His deliberation said:
translations.idiomatic: His written rebuttal ran as follows.

sentences.zh: 尚書稱「天秩有禮，五服五章哉。

translations.literal: The Shang shu says: 'Heaven's ordering has ritual; the five garments have five emblems.'
translations.idiomatic: The Documents praise Heaven's ordered ritual and the five insignia of rank.

sentences.zh: 天討有罪，五刑五用哉」。

translations.literal: 'Heaven punishes guilt; the five punishments have five applications.'"
translations.idiomatic: Heaven's punishments too have their proper fivefold use.

sentences.zh: 而孫卿亦云「凡制刑之本，將以禁暴惡，且懲其末也。

translations.literal: Sun Qing also says: 'In general the root of making punishments is to forbid violence and evil, and moreover to warn those who follow.'
translations.idiomatic: Xunzi says punishment exists to stop violence and warn the wicked.

sentences.zh: 凡爵列﹑官秩﹑賞慶﹑刑威，皆以類相從，使當其實也」。

translations.literal: 'In general noble ranks, official grades, rewards, and penal awe all follow kind so that each matches its reality.'"
translations.idiomatic: Ranks, offices, rewards, and terrors must each fit the deed.

sentences.zh: 若德不副位，能不稱官，賞不酬功，刑不應罪，不祥莫大焉。

translations.literal: If virtue does not match position, ability does not match office, reward does not repay merit, or punishment does not answer guilt, there is no greater ill omen.
translations.idiomatic: When virtue, talent, reward, and penalty fall out of alignment, the state courts disaster.

sentences.zh: 殺人者死，傷人者刑，此百王之定制，有法之成科。

translations.literal: Murderers die and wounders suffer mutilation—this is the fixed system of the hundred kings and the completed statute of law.
translations.idiomatic: Killers die and maimers are maimed: that code stood for every dynasty.

sentences.zh: 高祖入關，雖尚約法，然殺人者死，亦無寬降。

translations.literal: When the Exalted Ancestor entered the passes, although he honored simplified law, yet murderers died and there was no loosening of that.
translations.idiomatic: Even Gaozu's simplified Qin laws kept capital punishment for homicide.

sentences.zh: 夫時化則刑重，時亂則刑輕。

translations.literal: When the age is transformed punishments are heavy; when the age is chaotic punishments are light.
translations.idiomatic: Stable times call for stern justice; chaotic times may temper severity.

sentences.zh: [一]書曰「刑罰時輕時重」，此之謂也。

translations.literal: [1] The Documents says: 'Punishments are at times light, at times heavy'—this is what is meant.'
translations.idiomatic: [1] The Documents already taught flexible severity—within bounds.

sentences.zh: 注[一]犯化之罪固重，犯亂之罪為輕。

translations.literal: Note [1] Crimes committed in a transformed age are indeed heavy; crimes committed in a chaotic age are treated lightly.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The gloss explains the Documents line about matching penalty to the age.

sentences.zh: 今次﹑玉公以清時釋其私憾，阻兵安忍，殭屍道路。

translations.literal: Now Ci and Yu, in a clear age, vented private hatred, relied on arms and bore cruelty with ease, and left corpses in the road.
translations.idiomatic: Yet Yin Ci and Shi Yu had murdered in peacetime out of private spite.

sentences.zh: [一]朝恩在寬，幸至冬獄，而初﹑軍愚狷，妄自投斃。

translations.literal: [1] Court grace leaned toward leniency and they luckily reached the winter review of sentences, yet Chu and Jun were foolish and stubborn and rashly threw away their own lives.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Their relatives had volunteered to die in winter review, not to commute the convicts' guilt.

sentences.zh: 昔召忽親死子糾之難，而孔子曰「經於溝瀆，人莫之知」。

translations.literal: Formerly Shao Hu died in person for Prince Jiu's disaster, yet Confucius said, 'He strangled himself in a ditch—no one knows him.'
translations.idiomatic: He compared them to Shao Hu's useless suicide for Prince Jiu, which Confucius scorned.

sentences.zh: [二]朝氏之父非錯刻峻，遂能自隕其命，班固亦云「不如趙母指括以全其宗」。

translations.literal: [2] The father of Chao Cuo was not like Chao Ke's harsh carving—yet he was able to destroy his own life; Ban Gu also said, 'He was not like Mother Zhao, who pointed at Kuo to save her clan.'
translations.idiomatic: [2] He contrasted them with Chao Cuo's father and Zhao Kuo's mother, who died or intervened to save the law.

sentences.zh: [三]傳曰「僕妾感慨而致死者，非能義勇，顧無慮耳」。

translations.literal: [3] The tradition says: 'When slaves and concubines hang themselves out of emotion, it is not courage in duty—they simply lack forethought.'
translations.idiomatic: [3] Servants who hang themselves act from impulse, not from moral heroism.

sentences.zh: [四]夫刑罰威獄，以類天之震耀殺戮也；

translations.literal: [4] Punishments, awesome prisons, are to match Heaven's thunderous killing;
translations.idiomatic: [4] Prisons echo Heaven's killing thunder;

sentences.zh: 溫慈和惠，以放天之生殖長育也。

translations.literal: gentle kindness and harmony are to imitate Heaven's engendering and nurturing.
translations.idiomatic: mercy mirrors Heaven's nurturing rain.

sentences.zh: [五]是故春一草枯則為□，秋一木華亦為異。

translations.literal: [5] Therefore if in spring one blade of grass withers it is taken as an omen; if in autumn one tree blossoms it is also taken as strange.
translations.idiomatic: [5] One withered blade in spring or one bloom in autumn were omens—

sentences.zh: 今殺無罪之初﹑軍，而活當死之次﹑玉，其為枯華，不亦然乎？

translations.literal: Now to kill the guiltless Chu and Jun and let live Ci and Yu who ought to die—is that not the same as withered grass and strange blossom?
translations.idiomatic: —so sparing murderers while executing innocent volunteers would invert those signs.

sentences.zh: 陳忠不詳制刑之本，而信一時之仁，遂廣引八議求生之端。

translations.literal: Chen Zhong did not examine the root of making punishments but trusted a moment's kindness, thereupon broadly citing the eight deliberations and threads by which to seek life.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Zhong had mistaken transient mercy for principle and stretched the eight mitigations too far.

sentences.zh: 夫親故賢能功貴勤賓，豈有次﹑玉當罪之科哉？

translations.literal: As for kin, old friends, worthies, merit, nobility, diligence, and guests—how can there be a clause fitting Ci and Yu's crimes?
translations.idiomatic: None of the eight mitigation categories covered common murder.

sentences.zh: [六]若乃小大以情，原心定罪，[七] 此為求生，非謂代死可以生也。

translations.literal: [6] If by large and small one follows circumstance and investigates mind to fix guilt, [7] this is to seek grounds for life—it does not mean that dying in another's place can give life.
translations.idiomatic: [6][7] Mitigating intent is not the same as trading innocent lives for guilty ones.

sentences.zh: 敗法亂政，悔其可追。

translations.literal: To defeat the law and throw government into chaos—can regret be pursued?
translations.idiomatic: Twisting the code would corrupt the state beyond repair.

sentences.zh: 劭凡為駁議三十篇，皆此類也。

translations.literal: Shao in all composed thirty refutation pieces—mostly of this kind.
translations.idiomatic: Ying Shao wrote thirty such forensic refutations in all.

sentences.zh: 注[一]阻，恃也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Zu means rely on.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Zu here means to lean on or trust in force.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰，□州吁「阻兵而安忍。」

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says, [lacuna] Zhou Xu 'relied on arms and bore cruelty with ease.'"
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo uses the phrase of Zhou Xu, who trusted his troops and cruelty.

sentences.zh: 注[二]召忽，齊大夫。

translations.literal: Note [2] Shao Hu was a grandee of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Shao Hu served as a Qi minister.

sentences.zh: 子糾，齊襄公之庶子也。

translations.literal: Prince Jiu was the bastard son of Duke Xiang of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Jiu was Duke Xiang of Qi's illegitimate son.

sentences.zh: 子糾與小白爭國，子糾被殺，召忽其傅也，遂死之。

translations.literal: Prince Jiu contended for the state with Xiaobai; when Jiu was killed, Hu, who was his tutor, thereupon died for him.
translations.idiomatic: When Jiu lost the succession struggle to Duke Huan, his tutor Shao Hu died with him.

sentences.zh: 論語孔子論召忽曰：「豈若匹夫匹婦之為諒也，自經於溝瀆而莫之知也。」

translations.literal: The Analects records Confucius judging Shao Hu: 'Was it like the stubbornness of common men and women, who strangle themselves in ditches and are unknown to anyone?'"
translations.idiomatic: Confucius called Shao Hu's suicide a pointless peasant gesture.

sentences.zh: 注[三]前書，□錯為御史大夫，改更律令，諸侯諠嘩。

translations.literal: Note [3] The Former Han: Chao Cuo was imperial counselor and changed statutes and commands, so the feudal lords clamored.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Chao Cuo's reforms stirred the kingdoms against the throne.

sentences.zh: 錯父聞而非之，曰：「劉氏安而□氏危矣。

translations.literal: Chao's father heard and condemned him, saying, 'The Liu house will be secure while the Chao house is in peril.'
translations.idiomatic: His father foresaw that meddling would destroy their clan.

sentences.zh: 」遂飲藥而死。

translations.literal: ' Thereupon he drank poison and died.'
translations.idiomatic: The old man poisoned himself in protest.

sentences.zh: 史記曰，趙母，趙將馬服君趙奢之妻，趙括之母也。

translations.literal: The Shiji says: Mother Zhao was the wife of general Lord Horse-clad Zhao She of Zhao—she was Zhao Kuo's mother.
translations.idiomatic: The Records introduce Zhao Kuo's mother, wife of Zhao She.

sentences.zh: 奢死，趙欲以括為將，母謂趙王曰：「王以為括如其父，父子異心，願王勿遣。

translations.literal: When She died, Zhao wished to make Kuo general; the mother told the king of Zhao, 'If you think Kuo is like his father, father and son have different hearts—I beg you not to send him.'
translations.idiomatic: When Zhao wanted Kuo to command, his mother warned that the son was not the father.

sentences.zh: 」王曰：「吾計決矣。

translations.literal: ' The king said, 'My mind is settled.'"
translations.idiomatic: The king brushed her aside.

sentences.zh: 」括母曰：「王終將之，即有不稱，妾得無隨乎？

translations.literal: ' Kuo's mother said, 'If in the end you send him, and there is failure, may I escape punishment?'"
translations.idiomatic: She extracted a promise that she would not share his punishment if he failed.

sentences.zh: 」王許諾。

translations.literal: ' The king granted it.'
translations.idiomatic: The king swore agreement.

sentences.zh: 及括敗，王以母先言，竟不誅也。

translations.literal: When Kuo was defeated, because the mother had spoken first, in the end she was not executed.
translations.idiomatic: After Changping the court spared her because she had protested on record.

sentences.zh: 而班固引之以為□錯贊詞。

translations.literal: Yet Ban Gu cited it as praise language for [lacuna] Chao.
translations.idiomatic: Ban Gu invoked her example in discussing Chao Cuo—though the text is damaged at one graph.

sentences.zh: 注[四]言僕妾之致死者，顧由無計慮耳。

translations.literal: Note [4] It says that when slaves and concubines cause death, it is only because they lack planning.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: The gloss stresses that such suicides stem from blind passion, not policy.

sentences.zh: 語見史記欒布傳贊也。

translations.literal: The language appears in the Shiji praise to Luan Bu.
translations.idiomatic: The same point appears in the Shiji encomium on Luan Bu.

sentences.zh: 注[五]左傳鄭大夫游吉之詞。

translations.literal: Note [5] These are words of grandee You Ji of Zheng in the Zuo Tradition.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: You Ji's Zuo Tradition remark is cited.

sentences.zh: 注[六]周禮小司寇職鄭司農曰：「親，宗室有罪先請也。

translations.literal: Note [6] Director of agriculture Zheng Zhong on the office of junior minister of justice says: 'Kin means when the imperial clan has guilt they are first consulted.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [6]: The Zhou li gloss explains kin among the eight deliberations.

sentences.zh: 故謂舊知也。

translations.literal: Therefore it means old acquaintances.
translations.idiomatic: Kin here includes former associates.

sentences.zh: 賢謂有德行者。

translations.literal: Worthy means those possessed of virtuous conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Worthy denotes moral character.

sentences.zh: 能謂有道蓺者。

translations.literal: Able means those possessed of arts and the Way.
translations.idiomatic: Able denotes technical and ritual competence.

sentences.zh: 功謂有大勳也。

translations.literal: Merit means those who have great achievements.
translations.idiomatic: Merit covers men of outstanding service.

sentences.zh: 貴謂若今墨綬，有罪先請也。

translations.literal: Noble means such as today's black-ribbon grandees—when guilty they are first consulted.
translations.idiomatic: Noble rank matches officials who must be consulted before punishment.

sentences.zh: 勤謂憔悴國事。

translations.literal: Diligence means those worn thin by state affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Diligence covers ministers exhausted in public toil.

sentences.zh: 賓謂二王后。」

translations.literal: Guest means the two kings who came after [Shang and Zhou].'"
translations.idiomatic: Guest covers the two honored descendant houses.

sentences.zh: 注[七]左傳曰：「小大之獄，雖不能察，必以情。

translations.literal: Note [7] The Zuo Tradition says: 'In large and small lawsuits, though I cannot examine each, I must handle them by sentiment.'"
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [7]: The Zuo line on judging by human circumstance is quoted.

sentences.zh: 」原心定罪，解見霍諝傳也。

translations.literal: 'Investigating mind to fix guilt' is explained in Hu Guang's biography.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss on intent-based judgment points to Hu Guang's treatment.

sentences.zh: 又刪定律令為漢儀，建安元年乃奏之。

translations.literal: He further abridged statutes and commands into Han precedents, and in Jianan 1 presented them.
translations.idiomatic: He condensed statutes into the Han ritual compendium and submitted it in the first year of Jianan.

sentences.zh: 曰：

translations.literal: It said:
translations.idiomatic: The memorial began:

sentences.zh: 夫國之大事，莫尚載籍。

translations.literal: Of the great affairs of state none is higher than the written record.
translations.idiomatic: No state business outweighs preserving the documentary record.

sentences.zh: 載籍也者，決嫌疑，明是非，[一]賞刑之宜，允獲厥中，俾後之人永為監焉。

translations.literal: Written records decide what is doubtful, make clear right and wrong, [1] fix what suits reward and punishment, truly hit the mean, and make later men forever take warning.
translations.idiomatic: Archives settle doubt, clarify justice, align rewards and penalties with the golden mean, and instruct posterity.

sentences.zh: 故膠*

translations.literal: Therefore Jiao*
translations.idiomatic: Therefore Chancellor Dong—

translations.literal: (east)
translations.idiomatic: The manuscript gloss marks east for a damaged place-name graph.

sentences.zh: **[西]*相董仲舒老病致仕，朝廷每有政議，數遣廷尉張湯親至陋巷，問其得失。

translations.literal: **[west]* Chancellor Dong Zhongshu, old and ill, retired; whenever the court debated policy it repeatedly sent Commandant of Justice Zhang Tang in person to his humble lane to ask what was gained or lost.'
translations.idiomatic: —Zhongshu retired ill, yet Zhang Tang still walked to his alley for legal advice.

sentences.zh: [二] 於是作春秋決獄二百三十二事，動以經對，言之詳矣。

translations.literal: [2] Thereupon he made Chunqiu case judgments in two hundred thirty-two items, always answering with the classics—he spoke in detail.
translations.idiomatic: [2] From that came 232 Spring-and-Autumn case rulings grounded in canonical text.

sentences.zh: 逆臣董卓，蕩覆王室，典憲焚燎，靡有孑遺，開闢以來，莫或茲酷。

translations.literal: The traitorous minister Dong Zhuo overturned the royal house; canons and statutes were burned until nothing survived—since the opening of the age none has been so cruel.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo's fires destroyed the Han law library as nothing in history had.

sentences.zh: [三]今大駕東邁，巡省許都，拔出險難，其命惟新。

translations.literal: [3] Now the great chariot moves east to inspect Xu capital, having escaped peril—its mandate is anew.
translations.idiomatic: [3] With the court removed to Xu, the mandate must be rebuilt from scratch.

sentences.zh: 臣累世受恩，榮祚豐衍，竊不自揆，貪少雲補，輒撰具律本章句﹑尚書舊事﹑廷尉板令﹑決事比例﹑司徒都目﹑五曹詔書[四]及春秋斷獄凡二百五十篇。

translations.literal: Your servant, having received favor for generations with blessings overflowing, ventured beyond his measure, greedy to add a little cloud of help, thereupon compiled the statutes' chapter-and-verse glosses, old affairs of the Shang shu, commandant's wooden slips, decided cases by precedent, director of works' master lists, edicts of the five bureaus [4] and Chunqiu judgments—two hundred fifty pieces in all.
translations.idiomatic: He bundled 250 documents spanning statutes, Documents precedents, commandant slips, case law, ministerial registers, and Spring-and-Autumn rulings.

sentences.zh: 蠲去復重，為之節文。

translations.literal: He cut away duplicates and overlap and arranged them in sections.
translations.idiomatic: He removed repetition and imposed a clear structure.

sentences.zh: [五] 又集駁議三十篇，以類相從，凡八十二事。

translations.literal: [5] Further he collected thirty refutation pieces, grouped by kind—eighty-two items.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Thirty forensic refutations grouped eighty-two topics.

sentences.zh: 其見漢書二十五，漢記四，[六]皆刪□潤色，以全本體。

translations.literal: Of these twenty-five appear in the Han shu, four in the Han ji, [6] all trimmed and polished to preserve the whole.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Twenty-five pieces echo the Han shu, four the Han ji, all edited for consistency.

sentences.zh: 其二十六，博采古今纓瑋之士，文章煥炳，德義可觀。

translations.literal: Twenty-six broadly gather brilliant writers of past and present—their writings shine and their virtue may be viewed.
translations.idiomatic: Twenty-six draw on notable authors old and new.

sentences.zh: 其二十七，臣所創造。

translations.literal: Twenty-seven your servant himself created.
translations.idiomatic: Twenty-seven fascicles are his own composition.

sentences.zh: 豈繄自謂必合道衷，[七]心焉憤邑，聊以藉手。

translations.literal: How could I alone claim they must match the heart of the Way, [7]—my heart is stifled; I only lend a hand.
translations.idiomatic: He disclaimed perfection yet offered the work as a tool for the throne.

sentences.zh: [八]昔鄭人以干鼠為璞，鬻之於周；

translations.literal: [8] Formerly men of Zheng sold dried rats as jade and hawked them in Zhou;
translations.idiomatic: [8] He likened himself to the fool who sold sun-dried rats as gems.

sentences.zh: 宋愚夫亦寶燕石，緹□十重。

translations.literal: A stupid fellow of Song also treasured Yan stone and wrapped it in ten layers of damask [lacuna].
translations.idiomatic: The Song rustic who cherished worthless Yan stone in silken wraps is a parallel.

sentences.zh: 夫鶯之者掩口盧胡而笑，斯文之族，無乃類旃。

translations.literal: Those who heard it covered their mouths and laughed—this sort of writing, is it not of the same kind?
translations.idiomatic: Listeners laughed behind their sleeves at such pedantry.

sentences.zh: [九]左氏實雲雖有姬姜絲麻，不□憔悴菅蒯，蓋所以代匱也。

translations.literal: [9] The Zuo Tradition truly says: 'Though there be fine silks of Ji and Jiang, one does not [lacuna] wear coarse jian and kuai—thus they supply want.'
translations.idiomatic: [9] The Zuo line prefers plain utility over vain splendor.

sentences.zh: [一〇]是用敢露頑才，廁於明哲之末。

translations.literal: [10] Therefore I dare expose my stubborn talent at the tail of the wise.
translations.idiomatic: [10] So he humbly offered his compilation to wiser men.

sentences.zh: 雖未足綱紀國體，宣洽時雍，庶幾觀察，增闡聖聽。

translations.literal: Though it is not enough to bind the body of the state or spread timely harmony, perhaps in observation it may widen sagely hearing.
translations.idiomatic: It might not reorder the realm yet could broaden the emperor's counsel.

sentences.zh: 惟因萬機之餘暇，游意省覽焉。

translations.literal: May Your Majesty in the leisure after myriad tasks roam your mind and examine it.
translations.idiomatic: He begged the sovereign to read it in spare moments.

sentences.zh: 獻帝善之。

translations.literal: Emperor Xian praised it.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xian approved the work.

sentences.zh: 注[一]禮記曰：「夫禮者，決嫌疑，明是非。」

translations.literal: Note [1] The Record of Rites says: 'Rites decide what is doubtful and make clear right and wrong.'"
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The Liji defines ritual as clearing doubt.

sentences.zh: 注[二]事見前書。

translations.literal: Note [2] The matter appears in the Former Han.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Dong Zhongshu's consultations appear in the Han shu.

sentences.zh: 注[三]或，有也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Huo means 'there is.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Huo glosses as existence.

sentences.zh: 注[四]司徒即丞相也。

translations.literal: Note [4] The director of works is the chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: Here the minister of works stands for the chancellor.

sentences.zh: 總領綱紀，佐理萬機，故有都目。

translations.literal: He grasps the net and its mesh and assists in the myriad tasks—therefore there is a master list.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the office kept master registers of business.

sentences.zh: 成帝初置尚書員五人，漢舊儀有常侍曹﹑二千石曹﹑戶曹﹑主客曹﹑三公曹也。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Emperor Cheng's reign the Secretariat had five seats—the Han old precedents name attendant censor, two-thousand-bushel, household, guest reception, and three-dukes bureaus.
translations.idiomatic: Cheng's Secretariat divided into five bureaus listed in Han precedents.

sentences.zh: 注[五]復音復，重音直容反。

translations.literal: Note [5] Fu is read fu; chong is read with fanqie zhi-rong.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: Fanqie readings for duplicate graphs are given.

sentences.zh: 注[六]即東觀記。

translations.literal: Note [6] That is the Eastern Lodge record.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [6]: Han ji means the Eastern Lodge annals.

sentences.zh: 注[七]繄音烏兮反。

translations.literal: Note [7] Yi is read wu-xi.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [7]: Fanqie for yi.

sentences.zh: 繄猶是也。

translations.literal: Yi means 'this.'
translations.idiomatic: Yi here means 'thus' or 'this.'

sentences.zh: 注[八]藉音自夜反。

translations.literal: Note [8] Ji is read zi-ye.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [8]: Fanqie for ji.

sentences.zh: 注[九]尹文子曰：「鄭人謂玉未琢者為璞，周人謂鼠未臘者為璞。

translations.literal: Note [9] Yin Wenzi says: 'Men of Zheng call unworked jade pu; men of Zhou call unsmoked rats pu.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [9]: Yin Wenzi tells the pun on pu.

sentences.zh: 周人遇鄭賈，人曰：『欲買璞乎？

translations.literal: A man of Zhou met a Zheng merchant and said, 'Do you wish to buy pu?'
translations.idiomatic: The Zhou buyer asked the trader for pu.

sentences.zh: 』鄭賈曰：『欲之。

translations.literal: ' The Zheng merchant said, 'I wish it.'"
translations.idiomatic: The merchant agreed.

sentences.zh: 』出璞視之，乃鼠也，因謝不取。

translations.literal: ' He produced the pu to look—it was a rat, so he apologized and would not take it.'"
translations.idiomatic: He found a dried rat and walked away abashed.

sentences.zh: 」戰國策亦然。

translations.literal: ' The Stratagems of the Warring States says the same.
translations.idiomatic: The Zhanguo ce repeats the tale.

sentences.zh: 今此乃云「鄭人以干鼠為璞」，便與二說不同。

translations.literal: Now this says 'men of Zheng took dried rats as pu,' which differs from the two accounts.
translations.idiomatic: Ying Shao notes his wording differs from earlier versions.

sentences.zh: 此云「干鼠」，彼云「未臘」，事又差舛。

translations.literal: Here it says 'dried rat'; there it said 'unsmoked'—the facts again disagree.
translations.idiomatic: He flags textual variance between dried rat and unsmoked rat.

sentences.zh: 闕子曰：「宋之愚人得燕石梧台之東，歸而藏之，以為大寶。

translations.literal: Master Que says: 'A fool of Song found Yan stone east of Wutai and brought it home as a great treasure.'
translations.idiomatic: Master Que's story of the Yan stone opens.

sentences.zh: 周客聞而觀之，主人父齋七日，端冕之衣，釁之以特牲，革匱十重，緹巾十襲。

translations.literal: A guest of Zhou heard and wished to view it; the host's father fasted seven days, donned sacrificial robes, sacrificed a single victim, wrapped it in a leather box ten layers thick and damask tenfold.
translations.idiomatic: The host staged a ritual unveiling in brocade and leather cases.

sentences.zh: 客見之，俛而掩口盧胡而笑曰：『此燕石也，與瓦甓不殊。

translations.literal: When the guest saw it he bent down, covered his mouth, and laughed, saying, 'This is Yan stone—it differs in no way from roof tiles.'"
translations.idiomatic: The guest laughed that it was mere paving stone.

sentences.zh: 』主人父怒曰：『商賈之言，□匠之心。

translations.literal: ' The host's father angrily said, 'Merchant talk, [lacuna] artisan's heart.'"
translations.idiomatic: The host fumed at the insult.

sentences.zh: 』藏之愈固，守之彌謹。

translations.literal: ' He stored it more firmly and guarded it more strictly.'"
translations.idiomatic: He only hid the rock more zealously.

sentences.zh: 」旃，之也。

translations.literal: ' Zhan means 'it.'"
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: zhan is a pronoun.

sentences.zh: □音襲。

translations.literal: [lacuna] is read xi.
translations.idiomatic: A damaged graph is read like xi.

sentences.zh: 緹，赤色繒也。

translations.literal: Ti is red silk.
translations.idiomatic: Ti denotes crimson silk.

sentences.zh: 楚詞曰：「襲英衣兮緹□。

translations.literal: The Songs of Chu says: 'Put on the bright coat with ti [lacuna].'"
translations.idiomatic: Chu ci mentions bright ti robes.

sentences.zh: 」謂鮮明之衣。

translations.literal: ' It means bright clothing.'
translations.idiomatic: The gloss explains vivid garments.

sentences.zh: 注[一〇]左傳曰：「詩云：『雖有絲麻，無□菅蒯。

translations.literal: Note [10] The Zuo Tradition says: 'The ode says: Though there be silk and hemp, we do not [lacuna] wear jian and kuai.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [10]: The Zuo quotes a lost ode on preferring coarse cloth.

sentences.zh: 雖有姬﹑姜，無□蕉萃。

translations.literal: Though there be daughters of Ji and Jiang, we do not [lacuna] the withered and worn.'
translations.idiomatic: Fine brides are contrasted with humble mates.

sentences.zh: 凡百君子，莫不代匱。

translations.literal: All you gentlemen—none fails to supply want in turn.'"
translations.idiomatic: The ode urges mutual aid among lords.

sentences.zh: 』」杜注云：「逸詩也。

translations.literal: ' Du's commentary says: 'It is a lost ode.'"
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu calls it a stray poem.

sentences.zh: 姬、姜，大國之女。

translations.literal: Ji and Jiang are daughters of great states.
translations.idiomatic: Ji and Jiang mean princesses of great houses.

sentences.zh: 蕉萃，陋賤之人。

translations.literal: Jiao cui means mean and lowly persons.
translations.idiomatic: Jiao cui glosses as humble folk.

sentences.zh: 」蕉萃﹑憔*

translations.literal: ' Jiao cui and qiao [lacuna]
translations.idiomatic: The text parallels jiao cui with qiao—

translations.literal: (cui)
translations.idiomatic: —the graph cui appears in parentheses.

sentences.zh: **[悴]*古字通。

translations.literal: **[cui]* are interchangeable in antiquity.'
translations.idiomatic: The variants are ancient graphic swaps.

sentences.zh: 二年，詔拜劭為袁紹軍謀校尉。

translations.literal: In the second year an edict appointed Shao colonel of strategy for Yuan Shao's army.
translations.idiomatic: In the second year he became Yuan Shao's strategic colonel.

sentences.zh: 時始遷都於許，舊章堙沒，書記罕存。

translations.literal: At that time the capital had just moved to Xu; old statutes were buried and letters rarely survived.
translations.idiomatic: With the move to Xu, precedents were lost and archives thin.

sentences.zh: 劭慨然歎息，乃綴集所聞，著漢官禮儀故事，凡朝廷制度，百官典式，多劭所立。

translations.literal: Shao sighed and thereupon stitched what he had heard into Han offices, rituals, and precedents—whatever concerned court institutions and officers' models was mostly set up by Shao.
translations.idiomatic: He compiled Han bureaucratic ritual; much of what survived was his work.

sentences.zh: 初，父奉為司隸時，並下諸官府郡國，各上前人像贊，劭乃連綴其名，錄為狀人紀。

translations.literal: Earlier, when his father Feng was metropolitan commandant, he ordered every office and commandery to submit portrait eulogies of former men; Shao linked their names and recorded them as Portraits of Men.
translations.idiomatic: He turned the portrait eulogies his father collected into a biographical register.

sentences.zh: 又論當時行事，著中漢輯序。

translations.literal: Further he discussed current affairs and wrote Middle Han compiled preface.
translations.idiomatic: He also wrote the Middle Han preface on recent events.

sentences.zh: 撰風俗通，以辯物類名號，釋時俗嫌疑。

translations.literal: He composed Comprehensive Meaning of Customs to distinguish things' classes and names and to explain seasonal vulgar doubts.
translations.idiomatic: His Fengsu tong sorted terminology and folk belief.

sentences.zh: 文雖不典，後世服其洽聞。

translations.literal: Though the writing is not canonical, later ages admire his wide learning.
translations.idiomatic: The prose is plain yet later scholars prize his erudition.

sentences.zh: 凡所著述百三十六篇。

translations.literal: In all his compositions number one hundred thirty-six pieces.
translations.idiomatic: His corpus totals 136 works.

sentences.zh: 又集解漢書，皆傳於時。

translations.literal: Further he collected commentary on the Han shu—all circulated in his time.
translations.idiomatic: His Han shu commentary also circulated widely.

sentences.zh: 後卒於鄴。

translations.literal: Later he died at Ye.
translations.idiomatic: He died at Ye.

sentences.zh: 弟子瑒﹑璩，並以文才稱。

translations.literal: His disciples Yang and Qu both were famed for literary talent.
translations.idiomatic: Students Yang and Qu won fame as writers.
Endnote marker one.

sentences.zh: 注[一]華嶠書曰：「劭弟珣，字季瑜，司空掾。

translations.literal: Note [1] Hua Qiao's book says: 'Shao's younger brother Xun, courtesy name Jiyu, was aide in the ministry of works.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Hua Qiao names brother Ying Xun.

sentences.zh: 珣生瑒。

translations.literal: Xun fathered Yang.'
translations.idiomatic: Xun was Yang's father.

sentences.zh: 」魏志曰「瑒字德璉，瑒弟璩字休璉，鹹以文章顯」也。

translations.literal: ' The Wei zhi says Yang styled Deqian and Qu styled Xiuqian—both distinguished in letters.'"
translations.idiomatic: The Wei chronicle gives their styles and literary fame.

sentences.zh: 中興初，有應嫗者，生四子而寡。

translations.literal: At the restoration there was a woman of Ying who bore four sons and was widowed.
translations.idiomatic: Early Eastern Han saw a widow of the Ying clan with four sons.

sentences.zh: 見神光照社，試探之，乃得黃金。

translations.literal: She saw divine light on the earth altar; probing it, she obtained yellow gold.
translations.idiomatic: Spirit light on the village altar revealed buried gold.

sentences.zh: 自是諸子宦學，並有才名，至瑒七世通顯。

translations.literal: From this her sons all entered office and study, each winning talent's name—down to Yang seven generations flourished.
translations.idiomatic: Her line rose for seven generations to Yang's eminence.
Endnote marker one.

sentences.zh: 注[一]應順，將作大匠；

translations.literal: Note [1] Ying Shun, superintendent of construction;
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The genealogy begins with Ying Shun.

sentences.zh: 子疊，江夏太守；

translations.literal: his son Die, grand administrator of Jiangxia;
translations.idiomatic: Die governed Jiangxia.

sentences.zh: 疊生郴，武陵太守；

translations.literal: Die fathered Chen, grand administrator of Wuling;
translations.idiomatic: Chen governed Wuling.

sentences.zh: 郴生奉，從事中郎；

translations.literal: Chen fathered Feng, staff supervisor;
translations.idiomatic: Feng served as staff supervisor.

sentences.zh: 奉生劭，車騎將軍掾；

translations.literal: Feng fathered Shao, aide to the chariot-and-horse general;
translations.idiomatic: Feng begat Ying Shao, aide to the general-in-chief.

sentences.zh: 劭弟珣，司空掾；

translations.literal: Shao's younger brother Xun, aide in the ministry of works;
translations.idiomatic: Xun served the ministry of works.

sentences.zh: 珣子瑒，曹操闢為丞相掾。

translations.literal: Xun's son Yang; Cao Cao summoned him as aide to the chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Ying Xun's son Ying Yang became an aide under Cao Cao's chancellery.

sentences.zh: 霍諝字叔智，魏郡鄴人也。

translations.literal: Huo Xu, courtesy name Shuzhi, was a man of Ye in Wei commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Xu, styled Shuzhi, came from Ye in Wei commandery.

sentences.zh: 少為諸生，明經。

translations.literal: In youth he was a student and understood the classics.
translations.idiomatic: As a young scholar he mastered the canon.

sentences.zh: 有人誣諝舅宋光於大將軍梁商者，以為妄刊章文，坐系洛陽詔獄，掠考困極。

translations.literal: Someone slandered Xu's maternal uncle Song Guang before Grand General Liang Shang, alleging that he had recklessly altered edict wording; he sat bound in the Luoyang edict prison and was tortured to the limit.
translations.idiomatic: A malice-bearer accused Song Guang, Xu's uncle, of falsifying imperial documents; Guang landed in the Luoyang edict prison under torture.

sentences.zh: 諝時年十五，奏記於商曰：

translations.literal: Xu was then fifteen sui and submitted a memorial to Shang, saying:
translations.idiomatic: At fifteen Huo Xu addressed Liang Shang in writing.

sentences.zh: 將軍天覆厚恩，愍舅光冤結，前者溫教許為平議，雖未下吏斷決其事，已蒙神明顧省之聽。

translations.literal: General, Heaven's canopy shows thick favor; you pitied uncle Guang's tangled wrong; earlier your kind instruction promised a fair hearing—though subordinates have not yet judged the affair, already we have received spirit-like attention and review.
translations.idiomatic: He thanked Liang Shang for promising a fair review of Guang's case.

sentences.zh: 皇天后土，寔聞德音。

translations.literal: August Heaven and sovereign Earth truly heard your virtuous words.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven and earth, he said, had heard the general's fairness.

sentences.zh: 竊獨踴躍，私自慶幸。

translations.literal: I alone leap for joy and privately count it fortune.
translations.idiomatic: He confessed his personal relief.

sentences.zh: 諝聞春秋之義，原情定過，赦事誅意，故許止雖弒君而不罪，趙盾以縱賊而見書。

translations.literal: I have heard the meaning of the Chunqiu: trace feeling to fix fault, forgive the deed and punish the intent—therefore Xu Zhi though regicide was not blamed, while Zhao Dun was written for letting the assassin go.
translations.idiomatic: He cited Spring and Autumn doctrine on intent versus deed, naming Xu Zhi and Zhao Dun.

sentences.zh: [一]此仲尼所以垂王法，漢世所宜遵前修也。

translations.literal: [1] This is why Zhongni handed down the kingly model and what Han ought to follow from former cultivation.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Confucius's method, he argued, should guide Han justice.

sentences.zh: 傳曰：「人心不同，譬若其面。

translations.literal: The tradition says: 'Human hearts differ, as do their faces.'
translations.idiomatic: He quoted the proverb that faces and hearts vary.

sentences.zh: 」[二]斯蓋謂大小窳隆丑美之形，至於鼻目觿竅毛髮之狀，未有不然者也。

translations.literal: ' [2] That speaks to the large and small, sunken and raised, ugly and fair in form—down to nose, eyes, openings, hair—none is not so.'
translations.idiomatic: [2] Physical variety, he added, mirrors moral variety.

sentences.zh: 情之異者，剛柔舒急倨敬之閒。

translations.literal: Where feeling differs lies between hard and soft, slack and urgent, proud and humble.
translations.idiomatic: Temperaments range from rigid to yielding.

sentences.zh: 至於趨利避害，畏死樂生，亦復均也。

translations.literal: As for running toward profit and avoiding harm, fearing death and delighting in life—these too are the same for all.
translations.idiomatic: Yet all men share instinct for survival and gain.

sentences.zh: 諝與光骨肉，義有相隱，言其冤濫，未必可諒，且以人情平論其理。

translations.literal: Xu and Guang are bone and flesh; duty includes mutual concealment; speaking of his injustice may not win belief—yet let human feeling weigh the principle.
translations.idiomatic: He admitted bias as kin yet asked Liang Shang to judge by common sense.

sentences.zh: 注[一]許止，許悼公之子名止也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Xu Zhi was the son named Zhi of Duke Dao of Xu.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Identifies Xu Zhi of the Xu Zhi case.

sentences.zh: 公羊傳曰：「冬，葬許悼公。

translations.literal: The Gongyang says: 'In winter they buried Duke Dao of Xu.'
translations.idiomatic: The Gongyang records Duke Dao's burial.

sentences.zh: 賊未討何以書葬？

translations.literal: The bandits are not yet punished—why record the burial?
translations.idiomatic: The classic asks why burial is recorded before the regicide is avenged.

sentences.zh: 不成乎弒也。

translations.literal: It does not count as regicide.'
translations.idiomatic: The answer: the act was not treated as murder.

sentences.zh: 許悼公是止進藥而殺，是以君子加弒焉。

translations.literal: Duke Dao of Xu was killed by Zhi presenting medicine—therefore the gentleman adds the word regicide.
translations.idiomatic: Zhi's fatal dose is labeled regicide in the text.

sentences.zh: 葬許悼公是君子之赦止。

translations.literal: To bury Duke Dao of Xu is the gentleman's forgiving Zhi.
translations.idiomatic: The burial line shows mercy toward Zhi.

sentences.zh: 赦止者，免止罪之辭也。

translations.literal: To forgive Zhi is wording that exempts Zhi from guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Forgiveness here means no criminal label.

sentences.zh: 」何休注云：「原止欲愈父之病，無害父之意，故赦之。

translations.literal: ' He Xiu's commentary says: 'Tracing Zhi's wish to heal his father, he had no mind to harm his father—therefore forgive him.'"
translations.idiomatic: He Xiu stresses filial intent.

sentences.zh: 」是原情定過也。

translations.literal: ' This is tracing feeling to fix fault.'"
translations.idiomatic: That illustrates judging by motive.

sentences.zh: 又曰：「晉史書趙盾弒其君。

translations.literal: It also says: 'The Jin scribe wrote that Zhao Dun assassinated his lord.'
translations.idiomatic: He turned to Zhao Dun's case.

sentences.zh: 趙盾曰：『天乎無辜，吾不弒君。

translations.literal: Zhao Dun said: 'O Heaven, I am innocent—I did not assassinate the lord.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Dun protested his innocence.

sentences.zh: 』太史曰：『爾為仁為義，人殺爾君而不討賊，此非弒君如何？

translations.literal: ' The grand scribe said: 'You are called benevolent and right—someone killed your lord yet you did not punish the villain—if this is not regicide, what is it?'
translations.idiomatic: The scribe answered that failing to avenge regicide is itself regicide.

sentences.zh: 』」此赦事誅意也。

translations.literal: '" This is forgiving the deed and punishing the intent.'
translations.idiomatic: That exemplifies punishing intent over deed.

sentences.zh: 注[二]左傳鄭子產謂子皮曰：「人心不同，譬如面焉。

translations.literal: Note [2] The Zuo Tradition: Zichan of Zheng said to Zipi: 'Human hearts differ, as faces do.'"
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Zichan's remark in the Zuo is cited.

sentences.zh: 吾豈敢謂子面如吾面乎？」

translations.literal: ' How dare I say your face is like my face?'"
translations.idiomatic: Zichan denied that all faces—or hearts—match.

sentences.zh: 光衣冠子孫，逕路平易，[一]位極州郡，日望征辟，亦無瑕穢纖介之累，無故刊定詔書，欲以何名？

translations.literal: Guang is a cap-and-gown descendant; his path is smooth and easy; [1] his rank reached the commandery level; he daily hoped for summons—he also had no flaw or speck of stain—without cause to carve and fix edict wording, what name could he seek?
translations.idiomatic: [1] Song Guang was a clean official who needed no forgery for career gain.

sentences.zh: 就有所疑，當求其便安，豈有觸冒死禍，以解細微？

translations.literal: If he had doubts, he ought to have sought what was convenient and safe—how could he touch deadly disaster to resolve a trifle?
translations.idiomatic: Had he doubted a text, he would have sought a safe fix, not capital crime.

sentences.zh: 譬猶療饑於附子，止渴於酖毒，未入腸胃，已絕咽喉，豈可為哉！

translations.literal: It is like curing hunger with aconite or thirst with poisoned wine—before it enters bowels and stomach, the throat is already cut—how can it be done!
translations.idiomatic: Forging an edict to solve a small problem was suicidal folly.

sentences.zh: [二]昔東海孝婦見枉不辜，幽靈感革，天應枯旱。

translations.literal: [2] Formerly the filial woman of Donghai suffered wrong though guiltless; dark spirits were moved and altered; Heaven answered with drought.
translations.idiomatic: [2] He cited the Donghai widow whose false conviction brought three years of drought.

sentences.zh: [三]光之所坐，情既可原，守闕連年，而終不見理。

translations.literal: [3] Guang's offense, as to feeling, can already be traced; he has waited at the palace gate year after year yet in the end is not given justice.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Guang's case deserved review, yet he languished unheard.

sentences.zh: 呼嗟紫宮之門，泣血兩觀之下，[四]傷和致災，為害滋甚。

translations.literal: He cries out at the Gate of Purple Taurus and weeps blood beneath the Two Towers; [4] harming harmony brings disaster—the harm grows extreme.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Injustice at court, he warned, skewed cosmic harmony.

sentences.zh: 凡事更赦令，不應復案。

translations.literal: Whenever affairs meet an amnesty order, they ought not be reinvestigated.
translations.idiomatic: Amnesties should not be reopened on whim.

sentences.zh: 夫以罪刑明白，尚蒙天恩，豈有冤謗無征，反不得理？

translations.literal: When guilt and punishment are clear, one still receives Heaven's grace—how can there be slander without proof yet the contrary not gain justice?
translations.idiomatic: Clear criminals win mercy sometimes; how much more a framed innocent?

sentences.zh: 是為刑宥正罪，戮加誣侵也。

translations.literal: This would be to forgive punishment for true crime and add execution to slanderous harm.
translations.idiomatic: That would spare the guilty and kill the wrongly accused.

sentences.zh: 不偏不黨，其若是乎？

translations.literal: Without partiality, without faction—is it not so?
translations.idiomatic: He appealed to impartial justice.

sentences.zh: 明將軍德盛位尊，人臣無二，言行動天地，舉厝移陰陽，誠能留神，沛然曉察，必有於公高門之福，[五]和氣立應，天下幸甚。

translations.literal: Illustrious General, your virtue is great and position honored; among ministers there is no second; speech and act move Heaven and Earth; raising and setting shift yin and yang—if you truly fix your mind, flowingly understand, you will surely have the blessing of Yu the duke's high gate; [5] harmonious qi will respond at once—under Heaven will be greatly fortunate.'
translations.idiomatic: [5] He flattered Liang Shang while begging him to act as the just minister Yu Gong once was.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謂遵依常轍，無所規求也。

translations.literal: Note [1] It means following the usual wheel-ruts without private designs.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Guang walked the straight path of office.

sentences.zh: 注[二]史記蘇秦曰：「饑人之所以饑而不食烏喙者，以其愈充腹而與餓死者同患也。

translations.literal: Note [2] Su Qin in the Shiji says: 'The reason a starving man will not eat aconite though hungry is that though it fills the belly it shares the peril of starving to death.'"
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Su Qin compared desperate remedies to poisoned food.

sentences.zh: 」附子﹑烏喙，根同而狀異也。

translations.literal: ' Aconite and aconite root share the same root but differ in form.'"
translations.idiomatic: The gloss notes aconite variants share one rhizome.

sentences.zh: 注[三]前書曰，東海有孝婦，少寡無子，養姑甚謹，姑欲嫁之，終不肯。

translations.literal: Note [3] The Former Han says: In Donghai there was a filial woman, young, widowed, without sons; she tended her mother-in-law with great care; the mother-in-law wished to remarry her, but she would not.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: The Donghai widow legend begins.

sentences.zh: 姑告□人曰：「孝婦養我勤苦，我老，久累丁壯。

translations.literal: The mother-in-law told [lacuna] people: 'The filial woman tends me with bitter toil; I am old and long burden her prime years.'
translations.idiomatic: The old woman told neighbors she felt a burden on her daughter-in-law.

sentences.zh: 」乃自經死。

translations.literal: ' Thereupon she hanged herself and died.'
translations.idiomatic: She hanged herself to free the younger woman.

sentences.zh: 姑女告吏曰：「婦殺我母。

translations.literal: The mother-in-law's daughter told the clerk: 'The woman killed my mother.'"
translations.idiomatic: A spiteful kinswoman accused the widow of murder.

sentences.zh: 」吏驗之急，孝婦自誣服，具獄上府，太守竟論殺婦。

translations.literal: ' The clerk pressed the investigation harshly; the filial woman falsely confessed; the full case went up to the grand administrator, who in the end judged and killed the woman.'
translations.idiomatic: Officials tortured a confession from the innocent widow and executed her.

sentences.zh: 郡中枯旱三年。

translations.literal: In the commandery it was dry drought for three years.
translations.idiomatic: Donghai then suffered three years of drought.

sentences.zh: 後太守至，自祭孝婦墓，天立大雨，歲熟。

translations.literal: Later a grand administrator arrived, personally sacrificed at the filial woman's tomb, Heaven immediately sent great rain, and the year ripened.
translations.idiomatic: A new magistrate sacrificed at her grave; rain fell and the harvest returned.

sentences.zh: 注[四]天有紫微宮，是上帝之所居也。

translations.literal: Note [4] Heaven has the Purple Taurus Palace—there the Supreme Lord dwells.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: The Purple Palace symbolizes the high throne.

sentences.zh: 王者立宮，像而為之。

translations.literal: The king establishes his palace, imitating it.
translations.idiomatic: The royal palace mirrored Heaven's court.

sentences.zh: 兩觀謂闕也。

translations.literal: The Two Towers mean the gate towers.
translations.idiomatic: The twin gate towers stood for the palace gate.

sentences.zh: 注[五]於公，東海人，為郡決曹，決獄平。

translations.literal: Note [5] Yu Gong was a man of Donghai, bureau clerk for decisions in the commandery; he judged prison cases fairly.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: Yu Gong of Donghai was a famously just sentencing clerk.

sentences.zh: 其閭門壞，父老共修之。

translations.literal: His lane gate was ruined; the elders together repaired it.
translations.idiomatic: Neighbors rebuilt his broken gate.

sentences.zh: 於公曰：「少高大閭門，令容駟馬蓋車。

translations.literal: Yu Gong said: 'Raise the lane gate a little higher so it may admit a four-horse carriage with canopy.'
translations.idiomatic: He asked them to build a tall gate fit for a minister's chariot.

sentences.zh: 我決獄多有陰德，子孫必有興者。

translations.literal: ' I judge many cases with hidden virtue—my descendants must rise.'"
translations.idiomatic: He prophesied honor for his line because of his secret justice.

sentences.zh: 」至子定國為丞相，孫永御史大夫。

translations.literal: ' When it reached his son Dingguo as chancellor and grandson Yong as imperial counselor.'"
translations.idiomatic: His son became chancellor and his grandson imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 商高諝才志，即為奏原光罪，由是顯名。

translations.literal: Shang prized Xu's talent and resolve and thereupon memorialized to pardon Guang's crime—thereby Xu became illustrious.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Shang, impressed, cleared Song Guang and made Huo Xu famous.

sentences.zh: 仕郡，舉孝廉，稍遷金城太守。

translations.literal: He served the commandery, was recommended filial and incorrupt, and gradually was promoted grand administrator of Jincheng.
translations.idiomatic: He rose from commandery service to governor of Jincheng.

sentences.zh: 性明達篤厚，能以恩信化誘殊俗，甚為羌胡所敬服。

translations.literal: By nature bright, penetrating, honest, and thick, he could transform and entice alien customs with favor and trust, and was greatly respected and submitted to by Qiang and Hu.
translations.idiomatic: As governor he won tribal peoples by kindness and was deeply respected.

sentences.zh: 遭母憂，自上歸行喪。

translations.literal: He met mourning for his mother and personally returned to observe mourning.
translations.idiomatic: He resigned to mourn his mother.

sentences.zh: 服闋，公車征，再遷北海相，入為尚書僕射。

translations.literal: When mourning ended, the summons carriage summoned him; he was promoted twice to chancellor of Beihai, entered as vice director of the secretariat.
translations.idiomatic: After mourning the court recalled him to Beihai chancellorship and then vice director of the Secretariat.

sentences.zh: 是時大將軍梁冀貴戚秉權，自公卿以下莫敢違啎。

translations.literal: At that time Grand General Liang Ji, honored kin, grasped power; from the dukes and ministers downward none dared disobey.
translations.idiomatic: Under Liang Ji's dictatorship no minister dared resist.

sentences.zh: 諝與尚書令尹勳數奏其事，又因陛見陳聞罪失。

translations.literal: Xu and Minister Secretariat Director Yin Xun repeatedly memorialized on the affair, and further used audience at the steps to state crimes and failures.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Xu and Yin Xun kept impeaching Liang Ji at audience.

sentences.zh: 及冀誅後，桓帝嘉其忠節，封鄴都亭侯。

translations.literal: When Ji was executed, Emperor Huan praised his loyal integrity and enfeoffed him as marquis of the Ye village.
translations.idiomatic: After Ji's fall Emperor Huan ennobled Huo Xu for loyalty.

sentences.zh: 前後固讓，不許。

translations.literal: He repeatedly firmly declined; it was not permitted.
translations.idiomatic: He tried to refuse the fief but the throne insisted.

sentences.zh: 出為河南尹，遷司隸校尉，轉少府﹑廷尉，卒官。

translations.literal: He went out as metropolitan governor of Henan, was promoted metropolitan commandant, transferred minister of the household, commandant of justice, and died in office.
translations.idiomatic: He capped his career as minister of justice and died in harness.

sentences.zh: 子鑈，安定太守。

translations.literal: His son Jun was grand administrator of Anding.
translations.idiomatic: His son Huo Jun governed Anding.

sentences.zh: 爰延字季平，陳留外黃人也。

translations.literal: Yuan Yan, courtesy name Jiping, was a man of Waihuang in Chenliu.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Yan, styled Jiping, came from Waihuang in Chenliu.

sentences.zh: 清苦好學，能通經教授。

translations.literal: He was pure, bitter, fond of learning, able to penetrate the classics and teach.
translations.idiomatic: He lived austerely, loved study, and taught the canon.

sentences.zh: 性質□，少言辭。

translations.literal: By nature he was honest [lacuna] and spoke few words.
translations.idiomatic: He was plain-spoken and laconic, though one graph in the text is damaged.

sentences.zh: 縣令隴西牛述好士知人，乃禮請延為廷掾，范丹為功曹，濮陽潛為主簿，[一]常共言談而已。

translations.literal: County magistrate Niu Shu of Longxi loved scholars and knew men; thereupon he courteously invited Yan as bureau clerk to the commandant, Fan Dan as merit assessor, and Puyang Qian as secretary; [1] they often conversed together, that was all.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Magistrate Niu Shu of Longxi hired him as commandant's clerk alongside Fan Dan and Puyang Qian for conversation and counsel.

sentences.zh: 後令史昭以為鄉嗇夫，仁化大行，人但聞嗇夫，不知郡縣。

translations.literal: Later clerk Shi Zhao made him village overseer; humane transformation went greatly; people only heard of the overseer and did not know the commandery or county.
translations.idiomatic: As village overseer his moral sway was so strong that locals knew his name above the county.

sentences.zh: 在事二年，州府禮請，不就。

translations.literal: In office two years, the province and commandery courteously summoned him; he did not go.
translations.idiomatic: After two years higher offices called him, but he stayed put.

sentences.zh: 桓帝時征博士，太尉楊秉等舉賢良方正，再遷為侍中。

translations.literal: In Emperor Huan's time he was summoned as erudite; Grand Commandant Yang Bing and others recommended him worthy and upright; he was promoted twice to palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Huan summoned him as erudite; Yang Bing nominated him worthy and upright, and he rose to palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 注[一]濮陽，姓也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Puyang is a surname.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: Puyang is the clan name, not the place.

sentences.zh: 帝游上林苑，從容問延曰：「朕何如主也？

translations.literal: The emperor toured the Shanglin Park and calmly asked Yan: 'What sort of ruler am I?'
translations.idiomatic: On a park outing Emperor Huan asked what kind of ruler he was.

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「陛下為漢中主。

translations.literal: ' He answered: 'Your Majesty is a middling ruler of Han.'"
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Yan answered that he was a middling Han sovereign.

sentences.zh: 」帝曰：「何以言之？

translations.literal: ' The emperor said: 'Why do you say that?'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked for his reasoning.

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「尚書令陳蕃任事則化，中常侍黃門豫政則亂，是以知陛下可與為善，可與為非。

translations.literal: ' He answered: 'When Minister Secretariat Director Chen Fan handles affairs there is transformation; when palace attendants and yellow gates anticipate government there is chaos—therefore I know Your Majesty can be led to good and can be led to evil.'"
translations.idiomatic: He said the realm thrived under Chen Fan and rotted under eunuch rule—so the emperor could go either way.

sentences.zh: 」[一]帝曰：「昔朱雲廷折欄檻，今侍中面稱朕違，敬聞闕矣。

translations.literal: ' [1] The emperor said: 'Formerly Zhu Yun in court broke the railing; now the attendant in person calls my fault—I respectfully hear the omission.'"
translations.idiomatic: [1] The emperor compared him to Zhu Yun's blunt courage.

sentences.zh: 」[二]拜五官中郎將，轉長水校尉，遷魏郡太守，征拜大鴻臚。

translations.literal: ' [2] He was appointed leader of court gentlemen for the five offices, transferred chief commandant of the long river, promoted grand administrator of Wei commandant, summoned as grand herald.
translations.idiomatic: [2] He then rose through military and civil posts to grand herald.

sentences.zh: 注[一]前書曰：「齊桓公，管仲相之則霸，豎貂輔之則亂。

translations.literal: Note [1] The Former Han says: 'Duke Huan of Qi with Guan Zhong as minister hegemoned; with Shu Diao assisting him he fell into chaos.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: The Han history compares good and evil ministers to Guan Zhong and Shu Diao.

sentences.zh: 可與為善，可與為惡，是謂中人。」

translations.literal: ' One may be led to good, one may be led to evil—this is called the middling man.'"
translations.idiomatic: That defines a middling ruler swayed by counselors.

sentences.zh: 注[二]朱雲字游。

translations.literal: Note [2] Zhu Yun's courtesy name was You.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Zhu Yun, styled You.

sentences.zh: 成帝時上書求見，曰：「今朝廷大臣，上不能匡主，下無以益人，臣願賜尚方斬馬□，斷佞臣一人，以勵其餘。

translations.literal: In Emperor Cheng's time he submitted a memorial asking audience, saying: 'Now the great ministers of court above cannot correct the ruler and below cannot benefit the people—I beg to be granted the Shangfang beheading saber to cut off one flattering minister and encourage the rest.'"
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Yun had begged Chengdi for the imperial executioner's blade to kill a favorite.

sentences.zh: 」上問曰：「誰也？

translations.literal: ' The emperor asked: 'Who?'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked the target's name.

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「安昌侯張禹。

translations.literal: ' He answered: 'Marquis Anchang Zhang Yu.'"
translations.idiomatic: He named Zhang Yu.

sentences.zh: 」上大怒曰：「小臣廷辱師傅，罪死不赦。

translations.literal: ' The emperor grew angry and said: 'A petty minister insults my tutor in court—death cannot pardon the crime.'"
translations.idiomatic: Chengdi raged that a junior had insulted his teacher.

sentences.zh: 」御史將雲下，雲□殿檻折。

translations.literal: ' The imperial counselor was about to lead Yun down; Yun grasped the palace railing and broke it.'
translations.idiomatic: As guards dragged him away he snapped the palace railing.

sentences.zh: 雲呼曰：「臣得從龍逢﹑比干游於地下足矣，未知朝廷如何耳！

translations.literal: Yun shouted: 'If I may follow Longfeng and Bigan in the underworld it is enough—I do not know what will become of the court!'
